{{Multiple Issues |Source citation = March 2024 |Original research = March 2024 |No equivocation = March 2024 |Wikify = March 2024 |Excessive content = March 2024 }} {{Shrine | Name = 川上山若宮八幡宮 | Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} {{ill|Tsu City|ja|津市}} Misugi Town, Kawakami 3498 | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|27|49.3|N|136|15|57.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}　{{ill|Empress Iwanohime|ja|磐之媛皇后}} | Founded = Emperor Richū's era (400–406 AD) | Rank = Unranked shrine; the main hall, being a shared shrine, is classified as a village shrine | Annual Festival = October 19 | Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri | Image = [[画像:川上山若宮八幡宮.JPG|200px]]
拝殿 | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mie Prefecture|label=川上山若宮八幡宮|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine''' (川上山若宮八幡宮) is a {{ill|Wakamiyahachiman Shrine|ja|若宮八幡宮}} located in Misugi Town, {{ill|Tsu City|ja|津市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its nicknames include “Kawakami-sama”, “Wakamiya-sama”, “Kawakami Hachiman-sama”, and “Wakamiya Hachiman-sama” among others. It is the oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine in Japan. == Shrine Legend == According to the shrine legend, in the fief of Empress Iwanohime (磐之媛皇后) in Iwano-go, Emperor {{ill|Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} carried out the development of the region. In the era of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇}} (early 5th century AD), the local residents, in commemoration of the virtues of Emperor Nintoku and Empress Iwanohime, chose this pure land of Kawakami at the foot of a sacred shugendō mountain overlooking the {{ill|Ise Plain|ja|伊勢平野}} to establish the shrine and enshrine their revered spirit, thereby creating Japan’s oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. The shrine was highly revered by the Ise provincial governor’s family, the {{ill|Kitabatake family|ja|北畠家}}. Out of such veneration, a castle was built in the neighboring village of Taki, and after donating sacred lands, the elder of the Toriya-o family was appointed as a permanent magistrate. The venerable Sasabe Kanta Yuka of the Okano main family was designated as the hereditary shrine priest with the newly conferred title of “Taiyū.” Initially, there were twelve Taiyū, and during the Tōdō domain period, there were eight. The Kanta Yuka shrine priest, entrusted with esoteric (yin-yang) management, held the authority to appoint and dismiss sumo-style guardians such as Inosuke and his counterparts. Furthermore, it is said that in order to preserve the shrine’s dignity, the Kitabatake clan installed barriers beyond the Okano priest family residence to prohibit general public worship. During the grand spring and autumn festivals, the Ise provincial governor himself would visit the shrine, and new policies were always implemented in accordance with divine will. Prior to the Edo period, in the era of shinbutsu shūgō (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine possessed many affiliated temples. Descendants of {{ill|Hira Sosei|ja|平宗清}}—namely, the Hioki, Fukuchi, and the three factions of Iga Tsuge Uenokata (from the Kitamura group)—revered the shrine as their tutelary deity. It is also a shrine of monthly pilgrimage, prayer, and vow fulfillment (including vows for vengeance) associated with a renowned Daoist master from Ichinoi of {{ill|Iga|ja|伊賀市}} (also linked with {{ill|Nabari City|ja|名張市}}), known as Kotarō (Hachiman) Chōja and his son. The lords of the Tōdō domain also regarded it as a prayer site and invariably visited during the grand spring and autumn festivals to offer their votive contributions. Figures such as {{ill|En no Ozunu|ja|役小角}}, Otani Yoshitsugu (大谷吉継), and the master swordsman of the {{ill|Kashima Shintō-ryū|ja|鹿島新當流}}, Tsukahara Bokuden, are said to have developed the secret kusarigama technique here. In the precincts, the shrine priest known as Kanta Yuka is also reputed to have transmitted the first sword to {{ill|Kitabatake Tomonori|ja|北畠具教}}. Moreover, renowned ascetics including Inryū Aisū Ikōsai ({{ill|Aisū Hisatada|ja|愛洲久忠}}), Yagyū Shigenaga, and Shinkage-ryū Yagyū Ishūsai ({{ill|Yagyū Munenori|ja|柳生宗厳}}) have visited this sacred site for their waterfall ascetic practices. Since the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, during the medieval era the shrine flourished as a central sacred site for shugendō in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}. Furthermore, the origins of the shrine trace back even further—to an era when the sacred shugendō mountains (beginning with Shugyengyō-san), Mount Hiraura, Mount Kyokuzan (the “Western Peak”), Mount Takami (the “Eastern Peak”), and the three peaks of the Kawakami Thirteen Peaks of {{ill|Hakusan|ja|白山}} (三畝) along with Gakunō-dō Mountain, Mount Karatani (洞谷山), Mount Kuroiwa, Mount Gomazan, Mount Kosumayama, Mount Kōren, Mount Butsurai, and Mount Hakuran—served as shintai (sacred mountains) with many stone altars scattered throughout, giving rise to widespread ancestral spirit worship in the regions of Ise, Iga, Yamato, and Kii. Within the shrine grounds, the Tengu Rock is said to be the site where {{ill|Emperor Hanayama|ja|花山天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}} practiced the Great Tengu Method in their aspiration to become Japan’s great tengu. == Alternate Shrine Legend 1 == According to an alternate legend, Ennyōbō Chintoku Shōnin (the shrine priest of this shrine and concurrently the priest of the Kitabatake Castle Hachiman Shrine) received a prophetic dream warning of an imminent conflagration. He learned that from Kaga Hakusan a total of eight shrines were to be established in Isshigun—seven associated with Kaga Hakusan and one including the shrine’s own sacred mountain. On the night of January 24 in the 22nd year of Tenmon (1553), he experienced a divine message in his dream: “By next February, Kaga Hakusan will be consumed by fire, so I must relocate to the land where Amaterasu resides before then.” He also observed the Big Dipper scattered across the southern sky. Though he wished to set out for Kaga immediately, the heavy winter in Hokuriku forced him to wait until the snow subsided. In early March, he visited Hakusan and engaged in a seven-day, seven-night prayer. On the seventh night, a divine message came; the following morning, he found seven gohei (sacred wand offerings) standing upright inside his travel case. Taking this as confirmation of his prophetic dream, he prostrated himself before the deity and set off home with his travel case on his back. While pausing in his hometown to visit his elderly mother, he rested his travel case on a roadside stone along the bank of {{ill|Unidegawa|ja|雲出川}} at Seto-ga-fuchi near his family castle. Suddenly, the case began to sway, and from it, seven white herons took flight. These herons were regarded as divine messengers. The number seven was seen as echoing the Big Dipper he had seen earlier. Moved by this sign, he resolved to build a Hakusan Shrine at the place where the seven white herons alighted. They descended at seven locations—the Forest of the Small Wa Wind God, the boundary at Iugawaguchi, Hatayano, Yamadano, the family castle, Takehara, and Iifukuda—and he simultaneously initiated the construction of shrine buildings at these sites. It is said that he then enshrined a portion of the spirit of Kaga Hakusan. This is known as the Legend of the Three White Heron Deities. == Alternate Shrine Legend 2 == Another tradition relates that the severed heads of {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子}}—who defied Emperor Nintoku and fled the capital in pursuit of Ise—and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女}}, were carried by retainers and enshrined here. According to this legend, their heads washed ashore at various locations such as Kawagami in Hakusan Town at Kawaguchi Seto, Hatayano in Hakusan Town, Yamato Soni Village, and Takehara Nakano in Misugi Town. Additionally, within the Fujiwara Chikakata legend—linked with Iga Kawakami (in Aoyama Town) and Mammi in Hakusan Town—a severed head is said to have floated upriver and drifted to the Kawakami area, borne by the Four Demons. These traditions reveal the complex, ancestral spirit–worship facet of the Wakamiyahachiman belief. Along the route, the {{ill|Ise Ondo|ja|伊勢音頭}} is sung with the words that if one offers a heartfelt wish to Kawakami-sama (Wakamiya-sama), even if it seems a little beyond reach, it will be granted. It is said that not only in Ise but also in Shima, Owari, Mikawa, Mino, Hida, Tōtōmi, Izu, Yamato, Ōmi, Echigo, Etchu, Echizen, Kii, and Rakuchū did the divine “sword edge” descend—bestowing blessings from both Ise and this shrine. == Ise Tea Origin Site, Fuji‑kō, and Flood Control == Kawakami is also famed as the origin of Ise tea, for along the shrine’s approach lies the site of the first tea grove (Chakubo) planted by the priest {{ill|Myōe|ja|明恵}} (also known as Kōben). The shrine priest family’s tea garden has existed since that time. In addition, the birthplace of the sixth-generation Fuji‑kō, {{ill|Shokugyōshinroku|ja|食行身禄}}, is located at the Shimizui in the parish area, and there is also the record of the previous monk Shōkō Nyūtei. Since the Edo period it has been said that three entities surpass Isshigun in renown: Shokugyōshinroku, Matsuura Takeshirō (松浦武四郎), and Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. • Saijima Hachibei; the Unide Irrigation Canal (in the Kōchaya area, where a branch shrine dedicated to the shrine’s spirit water exists); Manno Pond (founded by Prince Kōbō Daishi and Kūkai, later restored by the Hosokawa family). • Yamanaka Tametsuna; the Kōya Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Kōya, Isshigun); Matsui Magoemon; the Ise Miyagawa Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Miyagawa Town, Ise City); Tendai Shinseisō Shōnin; and others who played significant roles in flood control projects (in imitation of the legend of Emperor Nintoku)—a fact that accounts for the many branch shrines dedicated to flood control deities in Owari, Mino, Iga, and Mikawa. == Return Stone == If one applies at the shrine office, prayers for the fulfillment of heartfelt wishes, longevity, good fortune, well-being of wells, and assured victory over one’s enemies are offered. In return, “sacred stones” are bestowed for enshrinement in household altars and wells, and upon the fulfillment of the prayer, a ritual is performed in which the number of stones is doubled—this “Return Stone” ritual has been carried out since ancient times. == Related Persons == 34th-generation shrine priest: the late Hirokatsu Okano, the eldest son of his wife Kei. 35th-generation shrine priest: the late Yamato Okano (brother of {{ill|Hirohiko Okano|ja|岡野弘彦}}), who was born at this shrine. {{stub}} == Enshrined Deities == 【Main Deity】 • {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} (Great Tsubame Deity) • {{ill|Empress Iwanohime|ja|磐之媛皇后}} (Katsuragi Iwanohime) 【Secondary Deities】 • {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子}} and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女}} • Hachinaga-hime • Katsuragi Osatsuhiko • {{ill|Takeuchi no Sukune|ja|武内宿禰}}-no-mikoto • Yorihime Ogara Sukune-no-mikoto 【Co-Enshrinement】 • Second Hall: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊}}; with secondary enshrinement of {{ill|Amaterasu Omikami|ja|大日孁貴神}} • Third Hall: {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} and the Hachiman Deity ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} and his aspect {{ill|Hota Wake|ja|誉田別尊}}) 【Subsidiary Shrines (Sessha)】 • Takamiya: {{ill|Ise Ōkunidama-no-Okami|ja|伊勢大国魂大神}} (Ise Tsuhiko-no-Kami) • Branch Shrines:  – Kitofusha: honoring figures such as Kitabatake Akeno-kō, Tōdō Kōji-kō, and Inari  – Michibiki Inari: associated with successive generations of the Osaki family (including the lineage of the current shrine head’s maternal Kubokou family and, ultimately, the last head priest of Kyoto Yasaka Shrine)  – Sakayakakino Yama-no-Kami, Misaka Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Hiurasaka); Sano-ya Yama-no-Kami (originally from Nakano); Tetsurakae Yama-no-Kami (originally from Aichi); and Kanayama Hiko-no-Mikoto • Fudansha: enshrining Tentsuibun Ōkami, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾尊}}, and the purification waterfall (the source of Unidegawa); also Tsukuyomi-sha, enshrining the deity Tsukuyomi • Fudōsha: honoring {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇大神}}, {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津比売神}}, {{ill|Hayaakitsuhiko/Hayaakitsuhime|ja|速開都比売神}}, {{ill|Kibikado Shukunokami|ja|気吹戸主神}} and {{ill|Haya-sasurahime|ja|速佐須良比売神}}, along with Izanami-no-Mikoto and the Harai Waterfall (also known as Utoru-taki or the Couple’s Waterfall); additionally, Fudōson as offered by Katsura in dedication • Komiyasha: enshrining Gofuku Hime-no-Mikoto, among others; and Fujishinroku-sha, honoring Konohanasa Kuya Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Aichi) • Tateishisha: enshrining {{ill|Yamimie-no-Kami|ja|闇龗神}} and {{ill|Takakagami|ja|高龗神}} • Manorikisha: enshrining {{ill|Tenmoku Ikka-no-Mikoto|ja|天目一箇命}} • Akagoishasha: enshrining {{ill|Nakisawa Megami|ja|泣沢女神}} and {{ill|Iwanagahime|ja|磐長姫尊}} == Main Treasures == The lantern stone associated with the tradition of Japanese filial piety (the “Twenty-Four Filial Exemplars”)—attributed to the tutelary deity Yamamoto Mokubei—is located in Nakamura Kurihara. The related lantern, once positioned before the Ota-ya, now stands in front of the Nino Torii parking lot, and the gourd field bestowed by the Tōdō domain lord is situated in Sakamoto. Also featured are the guardian dogs of the Komiyasha and those of the former Kitofusha (which enshrined deities such as {{ill|Kitabatake Akeno|ja|北畠顕能}} and {{ill|Tōdō Kōji|ja|藤堂高次}}). == External Links == * {{Official website|name=Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine Official Website|https://www.kawakamisanwakamiyahachiman.jp|format=flash}} * [https://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2929.html Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜Tourist Spot｜Tourism Mie] * [https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/918/ Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜See & Experience｜Official Tsu City Tourism Association website ...] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tsu City]] [[Category:Buildings in Tsu City]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Iwanaga Faith]]{{Iwanaga Faith}}[[Category:Iwanaga Faith]] [[ja:川上山若宮八幡宮]]

拝殿 | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mie Prefecture|label=川上山若宮八幡宮|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine''' (川上山若宮八幡宮) is a {{ill|Wakamiyahachiman Shrine|ja|若宮八幡宮}} located in Misugi Town, {{ill|Tsu City|ja|津市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its nicknames include “Kawakami-sama”, “Wakamiya-sama”, “Kawakami Hachiman-sama”, and “Wakamiya Hachiman-sama” among others. It is the oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine in Japan. == Shrine Legend == According to the shrine legend, in the fief of Empress Iwanohime (磐之媛皇后) in Iwano-go, Emperor {{ill|Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} carried out the development of the region. In the era of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇}} (early 5th century AD), the local residents, in commemoration of the virtues of Emperor Nintoku and Empress Iwanohime, chose this pure land of Kawakami at the foot of a sacred shugendō mountain overlooking the {{ill|Ise Plain|ja|伊勢平野}} to establish the shrine and enshrine their revered spirit, thereby creating Japan’s oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. The shrine was highly revered by the Ise provincial governor’s family, the {{ill|Kitabatake family|ja|北畠家}}. Out of such veneration, a castle was built in the neighboring village of Taki, and after donating sacred lands, the elder of the Toriya-o family was appointed as a permanent magistrate. The venerable Sasabe Kanta Yuka of the Okano main family was designated as the hereditary shrine priest with the newly conferred title of “Taiyū.” Initially, there were twelve Taiyū, and during the Tōdō domain period, there were eight. The Kanta Yuka shrine priest, entrusted with esoteric (yin-yang) management, held the authority to appoint and dismiss sumo-style guardians such as Inosuke and his counterparts. Furthermore, it is said that in order to preserve the shrine’s dignity, the Kitabatake clan installed barriers beyond the Okano priest family residence to prohibit general public worship. During the grand spring and autumn festivals, the Ise provincial governor himself would visit the shrine, and new policies were always implemented in accordance with divine will. Prior to the Edo period, in the era of shinbutsu shūgō (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine possessed many affiliated temples. Descendants of {{ill|Hira Sosei|ja|平宗清}}—namely, the Hioki, Fukuchi, and the three factions of Iga Tsuge Uenokata (from the Kitamura group)—revered the shrine as their tutelary deity. It is also a shrine of monthly pilgrimage, prayer, and vow fulfillment (including vows for vengeance) associated with a renowned Daoist master from Ichinoi of {{ill|Iga|ja|伊賀市}} (also linked with {{ill|Nabari City|ja|名張市}}), known as Kotarō (Hachiman) Chōja and his son. The lords of the Tōdō domain also regarded it as a prayer site and invariably visited during the grand spring and autumn festivals to offer their votive contributions. In the precincts, the shrine priest known as Kanta Yuka is also reputed to have transmitted the first sword to {{ill|Kitabatake Tomonori|ja|北畠具教}}. Moreover, renowned ascetics including Inryū Aisū Ikōsai ({{ill|Aisū Hisatada|ja|愛洲久忠}}), Yagyū Shigenaga, and Shinkage-ryū Yagyū Ishūsai ({{ill|Yagyū Munenori|ja|柳生宗厳}}) have visited this sacred site for their waterfall ascetic practices. Within the shrine grounds, the Tengu Rock is said to be the site where {{ill|Emperor Hanayama|ja|花山天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}} practiced the Great Tengu Method in their aspiration to become Japan’s great tengu. == Alternate Shrine Legend 1 == According to an alternate legend, Ennyōbō Chintoku Shōnin (the shrine priest of this shrine and concurrently the priest of the Kitabatake Castle Hachiman Shrine) received a prophetic dream warning of an imminent conflagration. Sano-ya Yama-no-Kami (originally from Nakano); Tetsurakae Yama-no-Kami (originally from Aichi); and Kanayama Hiko-no-Mikoto • Fudansha: enshrining Tentsuibun Ōkami, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾尊}}, and the purification waterfall (the source of Unidegawa); also Tsukuyomi-sha, enshrining the deity Tsukuyomi • Fudōsha: honoring {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇大神}}, {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津比売神}}, {{ill|Hayaakitsuhiko/Hayaakitsuhime|ja|速開都比売神}}, {{ill|Kibikado Shukunokami|ja|気吹戸主神}} and {{ill|Haya-sasurahime|ja|速佐須良比売神}}, along with Izanami-no-Mikoto and the Harai Waterfall (also known as Utoru-taki or the Couple’s Waterfall); additionally, Fudōson as offered by Katsura in dedication • Komiyasha: enshrining Gofuku Hime-no-Mikoto, among others; and Fujishinroku-sha, honoring Konohanasa Kuya Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Aichi) • Tateishisha: enshrining {{ill|Yamimie-no-Kami|ja|闇龗神}} and {{ill|Takakagami|ja|高龗神}} • Manorikisha: enshrining {{ill|Tenmoku Ikka-no-Mikoto|ja|天目一箇命}} • Akagoishasha: enshrining {{ill|Nakisawa Megami|ja|泣沢女神}} and {{ill|Iwanagahime|ja|磐長姫尊}} == Main Treasures == The lantern stone associated with the tradition of Japanese filial piety (the “Twenty-Four Filial Exemplars”)—attributed to the tutelary deity Yamamoto Mokubei—is located in Nakamura Kurihara. The related lantern, once positioned before the Ota-ya, now stands in front of the Nino Torii parking lot, and the gourd field bestowed by the Tōdō domain lord is situated in Sakamoto. Also featured are the guardian dogs of the Komiyasha and those of the former Kitofusha (which enshrined deities such as {{ill|Kitabatake Akeno|ja|北畠顕能}} and {{ill|Tōdō Kōji|ja|藤堂高次}}). == External Links == * {{Official website|name=Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine Official Website|https://www.kawakamisanwakamiyahachiman.jp|format=flash}} * [https://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2929.html Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜Tourist Spot｜Tourism Mie] * [https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/918/ Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜See & Experience｜Official Tsu City Tourism Association website ...] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tsu City]] [[Category:Buildings in Tsu City]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Iwanaga Faith]]{{Iwanaga Faith}}[[Category:Iwanaga Faith]] [[ja:川上山若宮八幡宮]] [[Category:New]]

{{Multiple Issues |Source citation = March 2024 |Original research = March 2024 |No equivocation = March 2024 |Wikify = March 2024 |Excessive content = March 2024 }} {{Shrine | Name = 川上山若宮八幡宮 | Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} {{ill|Tsu City|ja|津市}} Misugi Town, Kawakami 3498 | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|27|49.3|N|136|15|57.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}　{{ill|Empress Iwanohime|ja|磐之媛皇后}} | Founded = Emperor Richū's era (400–406 AD) | Rank = Unranked shrine; the main hall, being a shared shrine, is classified as a village shrine | Annual Festival = October 19 | Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri | Image = [[画像:川上山若宮八幡宮.JPG|200px]]
拝殿 | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mie Prefecture|label=川上山若宮八幡宮|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine''' (川上山若宮八幡宮) is a {{ill|Wakamiyahachiman Shrine|ja|若宮八幡宮}} located in Misugi Town, {{ill|Tsu City|ja|津市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its nicknames include “Kawakami-sama”, “Wakamiya-sama”, “Kawakami Hachiman-sama”, and “Wakamiya Hachiman-sama” among others. It is the oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine in Japan. == Shrine Legend == According to the shrine legend, in the fief of Empress Iwanohime (磐之媛皇后) in Iwano-go, Emperor {{ill|Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} carried out the development of the region. In the era of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇}} (early 5th century AD), the local residents, in commemoration of the virtues of Emperor Nintoku and Empress Iwanohime, chose this pure land of Kawakami at the foot of a sacred shugendō mountain overlooking the {{ill|Ise Plain|ja|伊勢平野}} to establish the shrine and enshrine their revered spirit, thereby creating Japan’s oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. The shrine was highly revered by the Ise provincial governor’s family, the {{ill|Kitabatake family|ja|北畠家}}. Out of such veneration, a castle was built in the neighboring village of Taki, and after donating sacred lands, the elder of the Toriya-o family was appointed as a permanent magistrate. The venerable Sasabe Kanta Yuka of the Okano main family was designated as the hereditary shrine priest with the newly conferred title of “Taiyū.” Initially, there were twelve Taiyū, and during the Tōdō domain period, there were eight. The Kanta Yuka shrine priest, entrusted with esoteric (yin-yang) management, held the authority to appoint and dismiss sumo-style guardians such as Inosuke and his counterparts. Furthermore, it is said that in order to preserve the shrine’s dignity, the Kitabatake clan installed barriers beyond the Okano priest family residence to prohibit general public worship. During the grand spring and autumn festivals, the Ise provincial governor himself would visit the shrine, and new policies were always implemented in accordance with divine will. Prior to the Edo period, in the era of shinbutsu shūgō (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine possessed many affiliated temples. Descendants of {{ill|Hira Sosei|ja|平宗清}}—namely, the Hioki, Fukuchi, and the three factions of Iga Tsuge Uenokata (from the Kitamura group)—revered the shrine as their tutelary deity. It is also a shrine of monthly pilgrimage, prayer, and vow fulfillment (including vows for vengeance) associated with a renowned Daoist master from Ichinoi of {{ill|Iga|ja|伊賀市}} (also linked with {{ill|Nabari City|ja|名張市}}), known as Kotarō (Hachiman) Chōja and his son. The lords of the Tōdō domain also regarded it as a prayer site and invariably visited during the grand spring and autumn festivals to offer their votive contributions. Figures such as {{ill|En no Ozunu|ja|役小角}}, Otani Yoshitsugu (大谷吉継), and the master swordsman of the {{ill|Kashima Shintō-ryū|ja|鹿島新當流}}, Tsukahara Bokuden, are said to have developed the secret kusarigama technique here. In the precincts, the shrine priest known as Kanta Yuka is also reputed to have transmitted the first sword to {{ill|Kitabatake Tomonori|ja|北畠具教}}. Moreover, renowned ascetics including Inryū Aisū Ikōsai ({{ill|Aisū Hisatada|ja|愛洲久忠}}), Yagyū Shigenaga, and Shinkage-ryū Yagyū Ishūsai ({{ill|Yagyū Munenori|ja|柳生宗厳}}) have visited this sacred site for their waterfall ascetic practices. Since the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, during the medieval era the shrine flourished as a central sacred site for shugendō in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}. Furthermore, the origins of the shrine trace back even further—to an era when the sacred shugendō mountains (beginning with Shugyengyō-san), Mount Hiraura, Mount Kyokuzan (the “Western Peak”), Mount Takami (the “Eastern Peak”), and the three peaks of the Kawakami Thirteen Peaks of {{ill|Hakusan|ja|白山}} (三畝) along with Gakunō-dō Mountain, Mount Karatani (洞谷山), Mount Kuroiwa, Mount Gomazan, Mount Kosumayama, Mount Kōren, Mount Butsurai, and Mount Hakuran—served as shintai (sacred mountains) with many stone altars scattered throughout, giving rise to widespread ancestral spirit worship in the regions of Ise, Iga, Yamato, and Kii. Within the shrine grounds, the Tengu Rock is said to be the site where {{ill|Emperor Hanayama|ja|花山天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}} practiced the Great Tengu Method in their aspiration to become Japan’s great tengu. == Alternate Shrine Legend 1 == According to an alternate legend, Ennyōbō Chintoku Shōnin (the shrine priest of this shrine and concurrently the priest of the Kitabatake Castle Hachiman Shrine) received a prophetic dream warning of an imminent conflagration. He learned that from Kaga Hakusan a total of eight shrines were to be established in Isshigun—seven associated with Kaga Hakusan and one including the shrine’s own sacred mountain. On the night of January 24 in the 22nd year of Tenmon (1553), he experienced a divine message in his dream: “By next February, Kaga Hakusan will be consumed by fire, so I must relocate to the land where Amaterasu resides before then.” He also observed the Big Dipper scattered across the southern sky. Though he wished to set out for Kaga immediately, the heavy winter in Hokuriku forced him to wait until the snow subsided. In early March, he visited Hakusan and engaged in a seven-day, seven-night prayer. On the seventh night, a divine message came; the following morning, he found seven gohei (sacred wand offerings) standing upright inside his travel case. Taking this as confirmation of his prophetic dream, he prostrated himself before the deity and set off home with his travel case on his back. While pausing in his hometown to visit his elderly mother, he rested his travel case on a roadside stone along the bank of {{ill|Unidegawa|ja|雲出川}} at Seto-ga-fuchi near his family castle. Suddenly, the case began to sway, and from it, seven white herons took flight. These herons were regarded as divine messengers. The number seven was seen as echoing the Big Dipper he had seen earlier. Moved by this sign, he resolved to build a Hakusan Shrine at the place where the seven white herons alighted. They descended at seven locations—the Forest of the Small Wa Wind God, the boundary at Iugawaguchi, Hatayano, Yamadano, the family castle, Takehara, and Iifukuda—and he simultaneously initiated the construction of shrine buildings at these sites. It is said that he then enshrined a portion of the spirit of Kaga Hakusan. This is known as the Legend of the Three White Heron Deities. == Alternate Shrine Legend 2 == Another tradition relates that the severed heads of {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子}}—who defied Emperor Nintoku and fled the capital in pursuit of Ise—and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女}}, were carried by retainers and enshrined here. According to this legend, their heads washed ashore at various locations such as Kawagami in Hakusan Town at Kawaguchi Seto, Hatayano in Hakusan Town, Yamato Soni Village, and Takehara Nakano in Misugi Town. Additionally, within the Fujiwara Chikakata legend—linked with Iga Kawakami (in Aoyama Town) and Mammi in Hakusan Town—a severed head is said to have floated upriver and drifted to the Kawakami area, borne by the Four Demons. These traditions reveal the complex, ancestral spirit–worship facet of the Wakamiyahachiman belief. Along the route, the {{ill|Ise Ondo|ja|伊勢音頭}} is sung with the words that if one offers a heartfelt wish to Kawakami-sama (Wakamiya-sama), even if it seems a little beyond reach, it will be granted. It is said that not only in Ise but also in Shima, Owari, Mikawa, Mino, Hida, Tōtōmi, Izu, Yamato, Ōmi, Echigo, Etchu, Echizen, Kii, and Rakuchū did the divine “sword edge” descend—bestowing blessings from both Ise and this shrine. == Ise Tea Origin Site, Fuji‑kō, and Flood Control == Kawakami is also famed as the origin of Ise tea, for along the shrine’s approach lies the site of the first tea grove (Chakubo) planted by the priest {{ill|Myōe|ja|明恵}} (also known as Kōben). The shrine priest family’s tea garden has existed since that time. In addition, the birthplace of the sixth-generation Fuji‑kō, {{ill|Shokugyōshinroku|ja|食行身禄}}, is located at the Shimizui in the parish area, and there is also the record of the previous monk Shōkō Nyūtei. Since the Edo period it has been said that three entities surpass Isshigun in renown: Shokugyōshinroku, Matsuura Takeshirō (松浦武四郎), and Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. • Saijima Hachibei; the Unide Irrigation Canal (in the Kōchaya area, where a branch shrine dedicated to the shrine’s spirit water exists); Manno Pond (founded by Prince Kōbō Daishi and Kūkai, later restored by the Hosokawa family). • Yamanaka Tametsuna; the Kōya Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Kōya, Isshigun); Matsui Magoemon; the Ise Miyagawa Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Miyagawa Town, Ise City); Tendai Shinseisō Shōnin; and others who played significant roles in flood control projects (in imitation of the legend of Emperor Nintoku)—a fact that accounts for the many branch shrines dedicated to flood control deities in Owari, Mino, Iga, and Mikawa. == Return Stone == If one applies at the shrine office, prayers for the fulfillment of heartfelt wishes, longevity, good fortune, well-being of wells, and assured victory over one’s enemies are offered. In return, “sacred stones” are bestowed for enshrinement in household altars and wells, and upon the fulfillment of the prayer, a ritual is performed in which the number of stones is doubled—this “Return Stone” ritual has been carried out since ancient times. == Related Persons == 34th-generation shrine priest: the late Hirokatsu Okano, the eldest son of his wife Kei. 35th-generation shrine priest: the late Yamato Okano (brother of {{ill|Hirohiko Okano|ja|岡野弘彦}}), who was born at this shrine. {{stub}} == Enshrined Deities == 【Main Deity】 • {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} (Great Tsubame Deity) • {{ill|Empress Iwanohime|ja|磐之媛皇后}} (Katsuragi Iwanohime) 【Secondary Deities】 • {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子}} and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女}} • Hachinaga-hime • Katsuragi Osatsuhiko • {{ill|Takeuchi no Sukune|ja|武内宿禰}}-no-mikoto • Yorihime Ogara Sukune-no-mikoto 【Co-Enshrinement】 • Second Hall: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊}}; with secondary enshrinement of {{ill|Amaterasu Omikami|ja|大日孁貴神}} • Third Hall: {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} and the Hachiman Deity ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} and his aspect {{ill|Hota Wake|ja|誉田別尊}}) 【Subsidiary Shrines (Sessha)】 • Takamiya: {{ill|Ise Ōkunidama-no-Okami|ja|伊勢大国魂大神}} (Ise Tsuhiko-no-Kami) • Branch Shrines:  – Kitofusha: honoring figures such as Kitabatake Akeno-kō, Tōdō Kōji-kō, and Inari  – Michibiki Inari: associated with successive generations of the Osaki family (including the lineage of the current shrine head’s maternal Kubokou family and, ultimately, the last head priest of Kyoto Yasaka Shrine)  – Sakayakakino Yama-no-Kami, Misaka Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Hiurasaka); Sano-ya Yama-no-Kami (originally from Nakano); Tetsurakae Yama-no-Kami (originally from Aichi); and Kanayama Hiko-no-Mikoto • Fudansha: enshrining Tentsuibun Ōkami, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾尊}}, and the purification waterfall (the source of Unidegawa); also Tsukuyomi-sha, enshrining the deity Tsukuyomi • Fudōsha: honoring {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇大神}}, {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津比売神}}, {{ill|Hayaakitsuhiko/Hayaakitsuhime|ja|速開都比売神}}, {{ill|Kibikado Shukunokami|ja|気吹戸主神}} and {{ill|Haya-sasurahime|ja|速佐須良比売神}}, along with Izanami-no-Mikoto and the Harai Waterfall (also known as Utoru-taki or the Couple’s Waterfall); additionally, Fudōson as offered by Katsura in dedication • Komiyasha: enshrining Gofuku Hime-no-Mikoto, among others; and Fujishinroku-sha, honoring Konohanasa Kuya Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Aichi) • Tateishisha: enshrining {{ill|Yamimie-no-Kami|ja|闇龗神}} and {{ill|Takakagami|ja|高龗神}} • Manorikisha: enshrining {{ill|Tenmoku Ikka-no-Mikoto|ja|天目一箇命}} • Akagoishasha: enshrining {{ill|Nakisawa Megami|ja|泣沢女神}} and {{ill|Iwanagahime|ja|磐長姫尊}} == Main Treasures == The lantern stone associated with the tradition of Japanese filial piety (the “Twenty-Four Filial Exemplars”)—attributed to the tutelary deity Yamamoto Mokubei—is located in Nakamura Kurihara. The related lantern, once positioned before the Ota-ya, now stands in front of the Nino Torii parking lot, and the gourd field bestowed by the Tōdō domain lord is situated in Sakamoto. Also featured are the guardian dogs of the Komiyasha and those of the former Kitofusha (which enshrined deities such as {{ill|Kitabatake Akeno|ja|北畠顕能}} and {{ill|Tōdō Kōji|ja|藤堂高次}}). == External Links == * {{Official website|name=Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine Official Website|https://www.kawakamisanwakamiyahachiman.jp|format=flash}} * [https://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2929.html Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜Tourist Spot｜Tourism Mie] * [https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/918/ Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜See & Experience｜Official Tsu City Tourism Association website ...] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tsu City]] [[Category:Buildings in Tsu City]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Iwanaga Faith]]{{Iwanaga Faith}}[[Category:Iwanaga Faith]] [[ja:川上山若宮八幡宮]] [[Category:New]]

拝殿 | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mie Prefecture|label=川上山若宮八幡宮|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine''' (川上山若宮八幡宮) is a {{ill|Wakamiyahachiman Shrine|ja|若宮八幡宮}} located in Misugi Town, {{ill|Tsu City|ja|津市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its nicknames include “Kawakami-sama”, “Wakamiya-sama”, “Kawakami Hachiman-sama”, and “Wakamiya Hachiman-sama” among others. According to this legend, their heads washed ashore at various locations such as Kawagami in Hakusan Town at Kawaguchi Seto, Hatayano in Hakusan Town, Yamato Soni Village, and Takehara Nakano in Misugi Town. Additionally, within the Fujiwara Chikakata legend—linked with Iga Kawakami (in Aoyama Town) and Mammi in Hakusan Town—a severed head is said to have floated upriver and drifted to the Kawakami area, borne by the Four Demons. These traditions reveal the complex, ancestral spirit–worship facet of the Wakamiyahachiman belief. Along the route, the {{ill|Ise Ondo|ja|伊勢音頭}} is sung with the words that if one offers a heartfelt wish to Kawakami-sama (Wakamiya-sama), even if it seems a little beyond reach, it will be granted. It is said that not only in Ise but also in Shima, Owari, Mikawa, Mino, Hida, Tōtōmi, Izu, Yamato, Ōmi, Echigo, Etchu, Echizen, Kii, and Rakuchū did the divine “sword edge” descend—bestowing blessings from both Ise and this shrine. == Ise Tea Origin Site, Fuji‑kō, and Flood Control == Kawakami is also famed as the origin of Ise tea, for along the shrine’s approach lies the site of the first tea grove (Chakubo) planted by the priest {{ill|Myōe|ja|明恵}} (also known as Kōben). The shrine priest family’s tea garden has existed since that time. In addition, the birthplace of the sixth-generation Fuji‑kō, {{ill|Shokugyōshinroku|ja|食行身禄}}, is located at the Shimizui in the parish area, and there is also the record of the previous monk Shōkō Nyūtei. Since the Edo period it has been said that three entities surpass Isshigun in renown: Shokugyōshinroku, Matsuura Takeshirō (松浦武四郎), and Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. • Saijima Hachibei; the Unide Irrigation Canal (in the Kōchaya area, where a branch shrine dedicated to the shrine’s spirit water exists); Manno Pond (founded by Prince Kōbō Daishi and Kūkai, later restored by the Hosokawa family). • Yamanaka Tametsuna; the Kōya Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Kōya, Isshigun); Matsui Magoemon; the Ise Miyagawa Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Miyagawa Town, Ise City); Tendai Shinseisō Shōnin; and others who played significant roles in flood control projects (in imitation of the legend of Emperor Nintoku)—a fact that accounts for the many branch shrines dedicated to flood control deities in Owari, Mino, Iga, and Mikawa. == Return Stone == If one applies at the shrine office, prayers for the fulfillment of heartfelt wishes, longevity, good fortune, well-being of wells, and assured victory over one’s enemies are offered. In return, “sacred stones” are bestowed for enshrinement in household altars and wells, and upon the fulfillment of the prayer, a ritual is performed in which the number of stones is doubled—this “Return Stone” ritual has been carried out since ancient times. == Related Persons == 34th-generation shrine priest: the late Hirokatsu Okano, the eldest son of his wife Kei. 35th-generation shrine priest: the late Yamato Okano (brother of {{ill|Hirohiko Okano|ja|岡野弘彦}}), who was born at this shrine. {{stub}} == Enshrined Deities == 【Main Deity】 • {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} (Great Tsubame Deity) • {{ill|Empress Iwanohime|ja|磐之媛皇后}} (Katsuragi Iwanohime) 【Secondary Deities】 • {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子}} and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女}} • Hachinaga-hime • Katsuragi Osatsuhiko • {{ill|Takeuchi no Sukune|ja|武内宿禰}}-no-mikoto • Yorihime Ogara Sukune-no-mikoto 【Co-Enshrinement】 • Second Hall: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊}}; with secondary enshrinement of {{ill|Amaterasu Omikami|ja|大日孁貴神}} • Third Hall: {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} and the Hachiman Deity ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} and his aspect {{ill|Hota Wake|ja|誉田別尊}}) 【Subsidiary Shrines (Sessha)】 • Takamiya: {{ill|Ise Ōkunidama-no-Okami|ja|伊勢大国魂大神}} (Ise Tsuhiko-no-Kami) • Branch Shrines:  – Kitofusha: honoring figures such as Kitabatake Akeno-kō, Tōdō Kōji-kō, and Inari  – Michibiki Inari: associated with successive generations of the Osaki family (including the lineage of the current shrine head’s maternal Kubokou family and, ultimately, the last head priest of Kyoto Yasaka Shrine)  – Sakayakakino Yama-no-Kami, Misaka Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Hiurasaka); Sano-ya Yama-no-Kami (originally from Nakano); Tetsurakae Yama-no-Kami (originally from Aichi); and Kanayama Hiko-no-Mikoto • Fudansha: enshrining Tentsuibun Ōkami, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾尊}}, and the purification waterfall (the source of Unidegawa); also Tsukuyomi-sha, enshrining the deity Tsukuyomi • Fudōsha: honoring {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇大神}}, {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津比売神}}, {{ill|Hayaakitsuhiko/Hayaakitsuhime|ja|速開都比売神}}, {{ill|Kibikado Shukunokami|ja|気吹戸主神}} and {{ill|Haya-sasurahime|ja|速佐須良比売神}}, along with Izanami-no-Mikoto and the Harai Waterfall (also known as Utoru-taki or the Couple’s Waterfall); additionally, Fudōson as offered by Katsura in dedication • Komiyasha: enshrining Gofuku Hime-no-Mikoto, among others; and Fujishinroku-sha, honoring Konohanasa Kuya Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Aichi) • Tateishisha: enshrining {{ill|Yamimie-no-Kami|ja|闇龗神}} and {{ill|Takakagami|ja|高龗神}} • Manorikisha: enshrining {{ill|Tenmoku Ikka-no-Mikoto|ja|天目一箇命}} • Akagoishasha: enshrining {{ill|Nakisawa Megami|ja|泣沢女神}} and {{ill|Iwanagahime|ja|磐長姫尊}} == Main Treasures == The lantern stone associated with the tradition of Japanese filial piety (the “Twenty-Four Filial Exemplars”)—attributed to the tutelary deity Yamamoto Mokubei—is located in Nakamura Kurihara. The related lantern, once positioned before the Ota-ya, now stands in front of the Nino Torii parking lot, and the gourd field bestowed by the Tōdō domain lord is situated in Sakamoto. Also featured are the guardian dogs of the Komiyasha and those of the former Kitofusha (which enshrined deities such as {{ill|Kitabatake Akeno|ja|北畠顕能}} and {{ill|Tōdō Kōji|ja|藤堂高次}}). == External Links == * {{Official website|name=Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine Official Website|https://www.kawakamisanwakamiyahachiman.jp|format=flash}} * [https://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2929.html Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜Tourist Spot｜Tourism Mie] * [https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/918/ Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜See & Experience｜Official Tsu City Tourism Association website ...] {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tsu City]] [[Category:Buildings in Tsu City]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Iwanaga Faith]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:川上山若宮八幡宮]]

{{Multiple Issues |Source citation = March 2024 |Original research = March 2024 |No equivocation = March 2024 |Wikify = March 2024 |Excessive content = March 2024 }} {{Shrine | Name = 川上山若宮八幡宮 | Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}} {{ill|Tsu City|ja|津市|12=draft}} Misugi Town, Kawakami 3498 | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|27|49.3|N|136|15|57.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}}　{{ill|Empress Iwanohime|ja|磐之媛皇后|12=draft}} | Founded = Emperor Richū's era (400–406 AD) | Rank = Unranked shrine; the main hall, being a shared shrine, is classified as a village shrine | Annual Festival = October 19 | Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri | Image = [[画像:川上山若宮八幡宮.JPG|200px]]
拝殿 | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mie Prefecture|label=川上山若宮八幡宮|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine''' (川上山若宮八幡宮) is a {{ill|Wakamiyahachiman Shrine|ja|若宮八幡宮|12=draft}} located in Misugi Town, {{ill|Tsu City|ja|津市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}. Its nicknames include “Kawakami-sama”, “Wakamiya-sama”, “Kawakami Hachiman-sama”, and “Wakamiya Hachiman-sama” among others. It is the oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine in Japan. == Shrine Legend == According to the shrine legend, in the fief of Empress Iwanohime (磐之媛皇后) in Iwano-go, Emperor {{ill|Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}} carried out the development of the region. In the era of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇|12=draft}} (early 5th century AD), the local residents, in commemoration of the virtues of Emperor Nintoku and Empress Iwanohime, chose this pure land of Kawakami at the foot of a sacred shugendō mountain overlooking the {{ill|Ise Plain|ja|伊勢平野|12=draft}} to establish the shrine and enshrine their revered spirit, thereby creating Japan’s oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. The shrine was highly revered by the Ise provincial governor’s family, the {{ill|Kitabatake family|ja|北畠家|12=draft}}. Out of such veneration, a castle was built in the neighboring village of Taki, and after donating sacred lands, the elder of the Toriya-o family was appointed as a permanent magistrate. The venerable Sasabe Kanta Yuka of the Okano main family was designated as the hereditary shrine priest with the newly conferred title of “Taiyū.” Initially, there were twelve Taiyū, and during the Tōdō domain period, there were eight. The Kanta Yuka shrine priest, entrusted with esoteric (yin-yang) management, held the authority to appoint and dismiss sumo-style guardians such as Inosuke and his counterparts. Furthermore, it is said that in order to preserve the shrine’s dignity, the Kitabatake clan installed barriers beyond the Okano priest family residence to prohibit general public worship. During the grand spring and autumn festivals, the Ise provincial governor himself would visit the shrine, and new policies were always implemented in accordance with divine will. Prior to the Edo period, in the era of shinbutsu shūgō (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine possessed many affiliated temples. Descendants of {{ill|Hira Sosei|ja|平宗清|12=draft}}—namely, the Hioki, Fukuchi, and the three factions of Iga Tsuge Uenokata (from the Kitamura group)—revered the shrine as their tutelary deity. It is also a shrine of monthly pilgrimage, prayer, and vow fulfillment (including vows for vengeance) associated with a renowned Daoist master from Ichinoi of {{ill|Iga|ja|伊賀市|12=draft}} (also linked with {{ill|Nabari City|ja|名張市|12=draft}}), known as Kotarō (Hachiman) Chōja and his son. The lords of the Tōdō domain also regarded it as a prayer site and invariably visited during the grand spring and autumn festivals to offer their votive contributions. Figures such as {{ill|En no Ozunu|ja|役小角|12=draft}}, Otani Yoshitsugu (大谷吉継), and the master swordsman of the {{ill|Kashima Shintō-ryū|ja|鹿島新當流|12=draft}}, Tsukahara Bokuden, are said to have developed the secret kusarigama technique here. In the precincts, the shrine priest known as Kanta Yuka is also reputed to have transmitted the first sword to {{ill|Kitabatake Tomonori|ja|北畠具教|12=draft}}. Moreover, renowned ascetics including Inryū Aisū Ikōsai ({{ill|Aisū Hisatada|ja|愛洲久忠|12=draft}}), Yagyū Shigenaga, and Shinkage-ryū Yagyū Ishūsai ({{ill|Yagyū Munenori|ja|柳生宗厳|12=draft}}) have visited this sacred site for their waterfall ascetic practices. Since the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, during the medieval era the shrine flourished as a central sacred site for shugendō in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}. Furthermore, the origins of the shrine trace back even further—to an era when the sacred shugendō mountains (beginning with Shugyengyō-san), Mount Hiraura, Mount Kyokuzan (the “Western Peak”), Mount Takami (the “Eastern Peak”), and the three peaks of the Kawakami Thirteen Peaks of {{ill|Hakusan|ja|白山|12=draft}} (三畝) along with Gakunō-dō Mountain, Mount Karatani (洞谷山), Mount Kuroiwa, Mount Gomazan, Mount Kosumayama, Mount Kōren, Mount Butsurai, and Mount Hakuran—served as shintai (sacred mountains) with many stone altars scattered throughout, giving rise to widespread ancestral spirit worship in the regions of Ise, Iga, Yamato, and Kii. Within the shrine grounds, the Tengu Rock is said to be the site where {{ill|Emperor Hanayama|ja|花山天皇|12=draft}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇|12=draft}} practiced the Great Tengu Method in their aspiration to become Japan’s great tengu. == Alternate Shrine Legend 1 == According to an alternate legend, Ennyōbō Chintoku Shōnin (the shrine priest of this shrine and concurrently the priest of the Kitabatake Castle Hachiman Shrine) received a prophetic dream warning of an imminent conflagration. He learned that from Kaga Hakusan a total of eight shrines were to be established in Isshigun—seven associated with Kaga Hakusan and one including the shrine’s own sacred mountain. On the night of January 24 in the 22nd year of Tenmon (1553), he experienced a divine message in his dream: “By next February, Kaga Hakusan will be consumed by fire, so I must relocate to the land where Amaterasu resides before then.” He also observed the Big Dipper scattered across the southern sky. Though he wished to set out for Kaga immediately, the heavy winter in Hokuriku forced him to wait until the snow subsided. In early March, he visited Hakusan and engaged in a seven-day, seven-night prayer. On the seventh night, a divine message came; the following morning, he found seven gohei (sacred wand offerings) standing upright inside his travel case. Taking this as confirmation of his prophetic dream, he prostrated himself before the deity and set off home with his travel case on his back. While pausing in his hometown to visit his elderly mother, he rested his travel case on a roadside stone along the bank of {{ill|Unidegawa|ja|雲出川|12=draft}} at Seto-ga-fuchi near his family castle. Suddenly, the case began to sway, and from it, seven white herons took flight. These herons were regarded as divine messengers. The number seven was seen as echoing the Big Dipper he had seen earlier. Moved by this sign, he resolved to build a Hakusan Shrine at the place where the seven white herons alighted. They descended at seven locations—the Forest of the Small Wa Wind God, the boundary at Iugawaguchi, Hatayano, Yamadano, the family castle, Takehara, and Iifukuda—and he simultaneously initiated the construction of shrine buildings at these sites. It is said that he then enshrined a portion of the spirit of Kaga Hakusan. This is known as the Legend of the Three White Heron Deities. == Alternate Shrine Legend 2 == Another tradition relates that the severed heads of {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子|12=draft}}—who defied Emperor Nintoku and fled the capital in pursuit of Ise—and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女|12=draft}}, were carried by retainers and enshrined here. According to this legend, their heads washed ashore at various locations such as Kawagami in Hakusan Town at Kawaguchi Seto, Hatayano in Hakusan Town, Yamato Soni Village, and Takehara Nakano in Misugi Town. Additionally, within the Fujiwara Chikakata legend—linked with Iga Kawakami (in Aoyama Town) and Mammi in Hakusan Town—a severed head is said to have floated upriver and drifted to the Kawakami area, borne by the Four Demons. These traditions reveal the complex, ancestral spirit–worship facet of the Wakamiyahachiman belief. Along the route, the {{ill|Ise Ondo|ja|伊勢音頭|12=draft}} is sung with the words that if one offers a heartfelt wish to Kawakami-sama (Wakamiya-sama), even if it seems a little beyond reach, it will be granted. It is said that not only in Ise but also in Shima, Owari, Mikawa, Mino, Hida, Tōtōmi, Izu, Yamato, Ōmi, Echigo, Etchu, Echizen, Kii, and Rakuchū did the divine “sword edge” descend—bestowing blessings from both Ise and this shrine. == Ise Tea Origin Site, Fuji‑kō, and Flood Control == Kawakami is also famed as the origin of Ise tea, for along the shrine’s approach lies the site of the first tea grove (Chakubo) planted by the priest {{ill|Myōe|ja|明恵|12=draft}} (also known as Kōben). The shrine priest family’s tea garden has existed since that time. In addition, the birthplace of the sixth-generation Fuji‑kō, {{ill|Shokugyōshinroku|ja|食行身禄|12=draft}}, is located at the Shimizui in the parish area, and there is also the record of the previous monk Shōkō Nyūtei. Since the Edo period it has been said that three entities surpass Isshigun in renown: Shokugyōshinroku, Matsuura Takeshirō (松浦武四郎), and Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. • Saijima Hachibei; the Unide Irrigation Canal (in the Kōchaya area, where a branch shrine dedicated to the shrine’s spirit water exists); Manno Pond (founded by Prince Kōbō Daishi and Kūkai, later restored by the Hosokawa family). • Yamanaka Tametsuna; the Kōya Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Kōya, Isshigun); Matsui Magoemon; the Ise Miyagawa Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Miyagawa Town, Ise City); Tendai Shinseisō Shōnin; and others who played significant roles in flood control projects (in imitation of the legend of Emperor Nintoku)—a fact that accounts for the many branch shrines dedicated to flood control deities in Owari, Mino, Iga, and Mikawa. == Return Stone == If one applies at the shrine office, prayers for the fulfillment of heartfelt wishes, longevity, good fortune, well-being of wells, and assured victory over one’s enemies are offered. In return, “sacred stones” are bestowed for enshrinement in household altars and wells, and upon the fulfillment of the prayer, a ritual is performed in which the number of stones is doubled—this “Return Stone” ritual has been carried out since ancient times. == Related Persons == 34th-generation shrine priest: the late Hirokatsu Okano, the eldest son of his wife Kei. 35th-generation shrine priest: the late Yamato Okano (brother of {{ill|Hirohiko Okano|ja|岡野弘彦|12=draft}}), who was born at this shrine. {{stub}} == Enshrined Deities == 【Main Deity】 • {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}} (Great Tsubame Deity) • {{ill|Empress Iwanohime|ja|磐之媛皇后|12=draft}} (Katsuragi Iwanohime) 【Secondary Deities】 • {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子|12=draft}} and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女|12=draft}} • Hachinaga-hime • Katsuragi Osatsuhiko • {{ill|Takeuchi no Sukune|ja|武内宿禰|12=draft}}-no-mikoto • Yorihime Ogara Sukune-no-mikoto 【Co-Enshrinement】 • Second Hall: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=draft}}; with secondary enshrinement of {{ill|Amaterasu Omikami|ja|大日孁貴神|12=draft}} • Third Hall: {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}} and the Hachiman Deity ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|12=draft}} and his aspect {{ill|Hota Wake|ja|誉田別尊|12=draft}}) 【Subsidiary Shrines (Sessha)】 • Takamiya: {{ill|Ise Ōkunidama-no-Okami|ja|伊勢大国魂大神|12=draft}} (Ise Tsuhiko-no-Kami) • Branch Shrines:  – Kitofusha: honoring figures such as Kitabatake Akeno-kō, Tōdō Kōji-kō, and Inari  – Michibiki Inari: associated with successive generations of the Osaki family (including the lineage of the current shrine head’s maternal Kubokou family and, ultimately, the last head priest of Kyoto Yasaka Shrine)  – Sakayakakino Yama-no-Kami, Misaka Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Hiurasaka); Sano-ya Yama-no-Kami (originally from Nakano); Tetsurakae Yama-no-Kami (originally from Aichi); and Kanayama Hiko-no-Mikoto • Fudansha: enshrining Tentsuibun Ōkami, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾尊|12=draft}}, and the purification waterfall (the source of Unidegawa); also Tsukuyomi-sha, enshrining the deity Tsukuyomi • Fudōsha: honoring {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇大神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津比売神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hayaakitsuhiko/Hayaakitsuhime|ja|速開都比売神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kibikado Shukunokami|ja|気吹戸主神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Haya-sasurahime|ja|速佐須良比売神|12=draft}}, along with Izanami-no-Mikoto and the Harai Waterfall (also known as Utoru-taki or the Couple’s Waterfall); additionally, Fudōson as offered by Katsura in dedication • Komiyasha: enshrining Gofuku Hime-no-Mikoto, among others; and Fujishinroku-sha, honoring Konohanasa Kuya Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Aichi) • Tateishisha: enshrining {{ill|Yamimie-no-Kami|ja|闇龗神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Takakagami|ja|高龗神|12=draft}} • Manorikisha: enshrining {{ill|Tenmoku Ikka-no-Mikoto|ja|天目一箇命|12=draft}} • Akagoishasha: enshrining {{ill|Nakisawa Megami|ja|泣沢女神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Iwanagahime|ja|磐長姫尊|12=draft}} == Main Treasures == The lantern stone associated with the tradition of Japanese filial piety (the “Twenty-Four Filial Exemplars”)—attributed to the tutelary deity Yamamoto Mokubei—is located in Nakamura Kurihara. The related lantern, once positioned before the Ota-ya, now stands in front of the Nino Torii parking lot, and the gourd field bestowed by the Tōdō domain lord is situated in Sakamoto. Also featured are the guardian dogs of the Komiyasha and those of the former Kitofusha (which enshrined deities such as {{ill|Kitabatake Akeno|ja|北畠顕能|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tōdō Kōji|ja|藤堂高次|12=draft}}). == External Links == * {{Official website|name=Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine Official Website|https://www.kawakamisanwakamiyahachiman.jp|format=flash}} * [https://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2929.html Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜Tourist Spot｜Tourism Mie] * [https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/918/ Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜See & Experience｜Official Tsu City Tourism Association website ...] {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tsu City]] [[Category:Buildings in Tsu City]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Iwanaga Faith]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:川上山若宮八幡宮]]

拝殿 | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mie Prefecture|label=川上山若宮八幡宮|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine''' (川上山若宮八幡宮) is a {{ill|Wakamiyahachiman Shrine|ja|若宮八幡宮|12=draft}} located in Misugi Town, {{ill|Tsu City|ja|津市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}. Its nicknames include “Kawakami-sama”, “Wakamiya-sama”, “Kawakami Hachiman-sama”, and “Wakamiya Hachiman-sama” among others. It is the oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine in Japan. == Shrine Legend == According to the shrine legend, in the fief of Empress Iwanohime (磐之媛皇后) in Iwano-go, Emperor {{ill|Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}} carried out the development of the region. In the era of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇|12=draft}} (early 5th century AD), the local residents, in commemoration of the virtues of Emperor Nintoku and Empress Iwanohime, chose this pure land of Kawakami at the foot of a sacred shugendō mountain overlooking the {{ill|Ise Plain|ja|伊勢平野|12=draft}} to establish the shrine and enshrine their revered spirit, thereby creating Japan’s oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. The shrine was highly revered by the Ise provincial governor’s family, the {{ill|Kitabatake family|ja|北畠家|12=draft}}. Out of such veneration, a castle was built in the neighboring village of Taki, and after donating sacred lands, the elder of the Toriya-o family was appointed as a permanent magistrate. The venerable Sasabe Kanta Yuka of the Okano main family was designated as the hereditary shrine priest with the newly conferred title of “Taiyū.” Initially, there were twelve Taiyū, and during the Tōdō domain period, there were eight. The Kanta Yuka shrine priest, entrusted with esoteric (yin-yang) management, held the authority to appoint and dismiss sumo-style guardians such as Inosuke and his counterparts. Furthermore, it is said that in order to preserve the shrine’s dignity, the Kitabatake clan installed barriers beyond the Okano priest family residence to prohibit general public worship. During the grand spring and autumn festivals, the Ise provincial governor himself would visit the shrine, and new policies were always implemented in accordance with divine will. Prior to the Edo period, in the era of shinbutsu shūgō (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine possessed many affiliated temples. Descendants of {{ill|Hira Sosei|ja|平宗清|12=draft}}—namely, the Hioki, Fukuchi, and the three factions of Iga Tsuge Uenokata (from the Kitamura group)—revered the shrine as their tutelary deity. It is also a shrine of monthly pilgrimage, prayer, and vow fulfillment (including vows for vengeance) associated with a renowned Daoist master from Ichinoi of {{ill|Iga|ja|伊賀市|12=draft}} (also linked with {{ill|Nabari City|ja|名張市|12=draft}}), known as Kotarō (Hachiman) Chōja and his son. The lords of the Tōdō domain also regarded it as a prayer site and invariably visited during the grand spring and autumn festivals to offer their votive contributions. Figures such as {{ill|En no Ozunu|ja|役小角|12=draft}}, Otani Yoshitsugu (大谷吉継), and the master swordsman of the {{ill|Kashima Shintō-ryū|ja|鹿島新當流|12=draft}}, Tsukahara Bokuden, are said to have developed the secret kusarigama technique here. In the precincts, the shrine priest known as Kanta Yuka is also reputed to have transmitted the first sword to {{ill|Kitabatake Tomonori|ja|北畠具教|12=draft}}. Moreover, renowned ascetics including Inryū Aisū Ikōsai ({{ill|Aisū Hisatada|ja|愛洲久忠|12=draft}}), Yagyū Shigenaga, and Shinkage-ryū Yagyū Ishūsai ({{ill|Yagyū Munenori|ja|柳生宗厳|12=draft}}) have visited this sacred site for their waterfall ascetic practices. Since the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, during the medieval era the shrine flourished as a central sacred site for shugendō in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}. Furthermore, the origins of the shrine trace back even further—to an era when the sacred shugendō mountains (beginning with Shugyengyō-san), Mount Hiraura, Mount Kyokuzan (the “Western Peak”), Mount Takami (the “Eastern Peak”), and the three peaks of the Kawakami Thirteen Peaks of {{ill|Hakusan|ja|白山|12=draft}} (三畝) along with Gakunō-dō Mountain, Mount Karatani (洞谷山), Mount Kuroiwa, Mount Gomazan, Mount Kosumayama, Mount Kōren, Mount Butsurai, and Mount Hakuran—served as shintai (sacred mountains) with many stone altars scattered throughout, giving rise to widespread ancestral spirit worship in the regions of Ise, Iga, Yamato, and Kii. Within the shrine grounds, the Tengu Rock is said to be the site where {{ill|Emperor Hanayama|ja|花山天皇|12=draft}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇|12=draft}} practiced the Great Tengu Method in their aspiration to become Japan’s great tengu. == Alternate Shrine Legend 1 == According to an alternate legend, Ennyōbō Chintoku Shōnin (the shrine priest of this shrine and concurrently the priest of the Kitabatake Castle Hachiman Shrine) received a prophetic dream warning of an imminent conflagration. He learned that from Kaga Hakusan a total of eight shrines were to be established in Isshigun—seven associated with Kaga Hakusan and one including the shrine’s own sacred mountain. On the night of January 24 in the 22nd year of Tenmon (1553), he experienced a divine message in his dream: “By next February, Kaga Hakusan will be consumed by fire, so I must relocate to the land where Amaterasu resides before then.” He also observed the Big Dipper scattered across the southern sky. Though he wished to set out for Kaga immediately, the heavy winter in Hokuriku forced him to wait until the snow subsided. In early March, he visited Hakusan and engaged in a seven-day, seven-night prayer. On the seventh night, a divine message came; the following morning, he found seven gohei (sacred wand offerings) standing upright inside his travel case. Taking this as confirmation of his prophetic dream, he prostrated himself before the deity and set off home with his travel case on his back. While pausing in his hometown to visit his elderly mother, he rested his travel case on a roadside stone along the bank of {{ill|Unidegawa|ja|雲出川|12=draft}} at Seto-ga-fuchi near his family castle. Suddenly, the case began to sway, and from it, seven white herons took flight. These herons were regarded as divine messengers. The number seven was seen as echoing the Big Dipper he had seen earlier. Moved by this sign, he resolved to build a Hakusan Shrine at the place where the seven white herons alighted. They descended at seven locations—the Forest of the Small Wa Wind God, the boundary at Iugawaguchi, Hatayano, Yamadano, the family castle, Takehara, and Iifukuda—and he simultaneously initiated the construction of shrine buildings at these sites. It is said that he then enshrined a portion of the spirit of Kaga Hakusan. This is known as the Legend of the Three White Heron Deities. == Alternate Shrine Legend 2 == Another tradition relates that the severed heads of {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子|12=draft}}—who defied Emperor Nintoku and fled the capital in pursuit of Ise—and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女|12=draft}}, were carried by retainers and enshrined here. According to this legend, their heads washed ashore at various locations such as Kawagami in Hakusan Town at Kawaguchi Seto, Hatayano in Hakusan Town, Yamato Soni Village, and Takehara Nakano in Misugi Town. Additionally, within the Fujiwara Chikakata legend—linked with Iga Kawakami (in Aoyama Town) and Mammi in Hakusan Town—a severed head is said to have floated upriver and drifted to the Kawakami area, borne by the Four Demons. These traditions reveal the complex, ancestral spirit–worship facet of the Wakamiyahachiman belief. Along the route, the {{ill|Ise Ondo|ja|伊勢音頭|12=draft}} is sung with the words that if one offers a heartfelt wish to Kawakami-sama (Wakamiya-sama), even if it seems a little beyond reach, it will be granted. It is said that not only in Ise but also in Shima, Owari, Mikawa, Mino, Hida, Tōtōmi, Izu, Yamato, Ōmi, Echigo, Etchu, Echizen, Kii, and Rakuchū did the divine “sword edge” descend—bestowing blessings from both Ise and this shrine. == Ise Tea Origin Site, Fuji‑kō, and Flood Control == Kawakami is also famed as the origin of Ise tea, for along the shrine’s approach lies the site of the first tea grove (Chakubo) planted by the priest {{ill|Myōe|ja|明恵|12=draft}} (also known as Kōben). The shrine priest family’s tea garden has existed since that time. In addition, the birthplace of the sixth-generation Fuji‑kō, {{ill|Shokugyōshinroku|ja|食行身禄|12=draft}}, is located at the Shimizui in the parish area, and there is also the record of the previous monk Shōkō Nyūtei. Since the Edo period it has been said that three entities surpass Isshigun in renown: Shokugyōshinroku, Matsuura Takeshirō (松浦武四郎), and Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. • Saijima Hachibei; the Unide Irrigation Canal (in the Kōchaya area, where a branch shrine dedicated to the shrine’s spirit water exists); Manno Pond (founded by Prince Kōbō Daishi and Kūkai, later restored by the Hosokawa family). • Yamanaka Tametsuna; the Kōya Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Kōya, Isshigun); Matsui Magoemon; the Ise Miyagawa Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Miyagawa Town, Ise City); Tendai Shinseisō Shōnin; and others who played significant roles in flood control projects (in imitation of the legend of Emperor Nintoku)—a fact that accounts for the many branch shrines dedicated to flood control deities in Owari, Mino, Iga, and Mikawa. == Return Stone == If one applies at the shrine office, prayers for the fulfillment of heartfelt wishes, longevity, good fortune, well-being of wells, and assured victory over one’s enemies are offered. In return, “sacred stones” are bestowed for enshrinement in household altars and wells, and upon the fulfillment of the prayer, a ritual is performed in which the number of stones is doubled—this “Return Stone” ritual has been carried out since ancient times. == Related Persons == 34th-generation shrine priest: the late Hirokatsu Okano, the eldest son of his wife Kei. 35th-generation shrine priest: the late Yamato Okano (brother of {{ill|Hirohiko Okano|ja|岡野弘彦|12=draft}}), who was born at this shrine. {{stub}} == Enshrined Deities == 【Main Deity】 • {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}} (Great Tsubame Deity) • {{ill|Empress Iwanohime|ja|磐之媛皇后|12=draft}} (Katsuragi Iwanohime) 【Secondary Deities】 • {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子|12=draft}} and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女|12=draft}} • Hachinaga-hime • Katsuragi Osatsuhiko • {{ill|Takeuchi no Sukune|ja|武内宿禰|12=draft}}-no-mikoto • Yorihime Ogara Sukune-no-mikoto 【Co-Enshrinement】 • Second Hall: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=draft}}; with secondary enshrinement of {{ill|Amaterasu Omikami|ja|大日孁貴神|12=draft}} • Third Hall: {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}} and the Hachiman Deity ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|12=draft}} and his aspect {{ill|Hota Wake|ja|誉田別尊|12=draft}}) 【Subsidiary Shrines (Sessha)】 • Takamiya: {{ill|Ise Ōkunidama-no-Okami|ja|伊勢大国魂大神|12=draft}} (Ise Tsuhiko-no-Kami) • Branch Shrines:  – Kitofusha: honoring figures such as Kitabatake Akeno-kō, Tōdō Kōji-kō, and Inari  – Michibiki Inari: associated with successive generations of the Osaki family (including the lineage of the current shrine head’s maternal Kubokou family and, ultimately, the last head priest of Kyoto Yasaka Shrine)  – Sakayakakino Yama-no-Kami, Misaka Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Hiurasaka); Sano-ya Yama-no-Kami (originally from Nakano); Tetsurakae Yama-no-Kami (originally from Aichi); and Kanayama Hiko-no-Mikoto • Fudansha: enshrining Tentsuibun Ōkami, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾尊|12=draft}}, and the purification waterfall (the source of Unidegawa); also Tsukuyomi-sha, enshrining the deity Tsukuyomi • Fudōsha: honoring {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇大神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津比売神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hayaakitsuhiko/Hayaakitsuhime|ja|速開都比売神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kibikado Shukunokami|ja|気吹戸主神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Haya-sasurahime|ja|速佐須良比売神|12=draft}}, along with Izanami-no-Mikoto and the Harai Waterfall (also known as Utoru-taki or the Couple’s Waterfall); additionally, Fudōson as offered by Katsura in dedication • Komiyasha: enshrining Gofuku Hime-no-Mikoto, among others; and Fujishinroku-sha, honoring Konohanasa Kuya Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Aichi) • Tateishisha: enshrining {{ill|Yamimie-no-Kami|ja|闇龗神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Takakagami|ja|高龗神|12=draft}} • Manorikisha: enshrining {{ill|Tenmoku Ikka-no-Mikoto|ja|天目一箇命|12=draft}} • Akagoishasha: enshrining {{ill|Nakisawa Megami|ja|泣沢女神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Iwanagahime|ja|磐長姫尊|12=draft}} == Main Treasures == The lantern stone associated with the tradition of Japanese filial piety (the “Twenty-Four Filial Exemplars”)—attributed to the tutelary deity Yamamoto Mokubei—is located in Nakamura Kurihara. The related lantern, once positioned before the Ota-ya, now stands in front of the Nino Torii parking lot, and the gourd field bestowed by the Tōdō domain lord is situated in Sakamoto. Also featured are the guardian dogs of the Komiyasha and those of the former Kitofusha (which enshrined deities such as {{ill|Kitabatake Akeno|ja|北畠顕能|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tōdō Kōji|ja|藤堂高次|12=draft}}). == External Links == * {{Official website|name=Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine Official Website|https://www.kawakamisanwakamiyahachiman.jp|format=flash}} * [https://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2929.html Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜Tourist Spot｜Tourism Mie] * [https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/918/ Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜See & Experience｜Official Tsu City Tourism Association website ...] {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tsu City]] [[Category:Buildings in Tsu City]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Iwanaga Faith]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:川上山若宮八幡宮]]

拝殿 | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mie Prefecture|label=川上山若宮八幡宮|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine''' (川上山若宮八幡宮) is a {{ill|Wakamiyahachiman Shrine|ja|若宮八幡宮|12=draft}} located in Misugi Town, {{ill|Tsu City|ja|津市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}. Its nicknames include “Kawakami-sama”, “Wakamiya-sama”, “Kawakami Hachiman-sama”, and “Wakamiya Hachiman-sama” among others. It is the oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine in Japan. == Shrine Legend == According to the shrine legend, in the fief of Empress Iwanohime (磐之媛皇后) in Iwano-go, Emperor {{ill|Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}} carried out the development of the region. In the era of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇|12=draft}} (early 5th century AD), the local residents, in commemoration of the virtues of Emperor Nintoku and Empress Iwanohime, chose this pure land of Kawakami at the foot of a sacred shugendō mountain overlooking the {{ill|Ise Plain|ja|伊勢平野|12=draft}} to establish the shrine and enshrine their revered spirit, thereby creating Japan’s oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. The shrine was highly revered by the Ise provincial governor’s family, the {{ill|Kitabatake family|ja|北畠家|12=draft}}. Out of such veneration, a castle was built in the neighboring village of Taki, and after donating sacred lands, the elder of the Toriya-o family was appointed as a permanent magistrate. The venerable Sasabe Kanta Yuka of the Okano main family was designated as the hereditary shrine priest with the newly conferred title of “Taiyū.” Initially, there were twelve Taiyū, and during the Tōdō domain period, there were eight. The Kanta Yuka shrine priest, entrusted with esoteric (yin-yang) management, held the authority to appoint and dismiss sumo-style guardians such as Inosuke and his counterparts. Furthermore, it is said that in order to preserve the shrine’s dignity, the Kitabatake clan installed barriers beyond the Okano priest family residence to prohibit general public worship. During the grand spring and autumn festivals, the Ise provincial governor himself would visit the shrine, and new policies were always implemented in accordance with divine will. Prior to the Edo period, in the era of shinbutsu shūgō (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine possessed many affiliated temples. Descendants of {{ill|Hira Sosei|ja|平宗清|12=draft}}—namely, the Hioki, Fukuchi, and the three factions of Iga Tsuge Uenokata (from the Kitamura group)—revered the shrine as their tutelary deity. It is also a shrine of monthly pilgrimage, prayer, and vow fulfillment (including vows for vengeance) associated with a renowned Daoist master from Ichinoi of {{ill|Iga|ja|伊賀市|12=draft}} (also linked with {{ill|Nabari City|ja|名張市|12=draft}}), known as Kotarō (Hachiman) Chōja and his son. The lords of the Tōdō domain also regarded it as a prayer site and invariably visited during the grand spring and autumn festivals to offer their votive contributions. Figures such as {{ill|En no Ozunu|ja|役小角|12=draft}}, Otani Yoshitsugu (大谷吉継), and the master swordsman of the {{ill|Kashima Shintō-ryū|ja|鹿島新當流|12=draft}}, Tsukahara Bokuden, are said to have developed the secret kusarigama technique here. In the precincts, the shrine priest known as Kanta Yuka is also reputed to have transmitted the first sword to {{ill|Kitabatake Tomonori|ja|北畠具教|12=draft}}. Moreover, renowned ascetics including Inryū Aisū Ikōsai ({{ill|Aisū Hisatada|ja|愛洲久忠|12=draft}}), Yagyū Shigenaga, and Shinkage-ryū Yagyū Ishūsai ({{ill|Yagyū Munenori|ja|柳生宗厳|12=draft}}) have visited this sacred site for their waterfall ascetic practices. Since the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, during the medieval era the shrine flourished as a central sacred site for shugendō in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}. Furthermore, the origins of the shrine trace back even further—to an era when the sacred shugendō mountains (beginning with Shugyengyō-san), Mount Hiraura, Mount Kyokuzan (the “Western Peak”), Mount Takami (the “Eastern Peak”), and the three peaks of the Kawakami Thirteen Peaks of {{ill|Hakusan|ja|白山|12=draft}} (三畝) along with Gakunō-dō Mountain, Mount Karatani (洞谷山), Mount Kuroiwa, Mount Gomazan, Mount Kosumayama, Mount Kōren, Mount Butsurai, and Mount Hakuran—served as shintai (sacred mountains) with many stone altars scattered throughout, giving rise to widespread ancestral spirit worship in the regions of Ise, Iga, Yamato, and Kii.
