In return, “sacred stones” are bestowed for enshrinement in household altars and wells, and upon the fulfillment of the prayer, a ritual is performed in which the number of stones is doubled—this “Return Stone” ritual has been carried out since ancient times. == Related Persons == 34th-generation shrine priest: the late Hirokatsu Okano, the eldest son of his wife Kei. 35th-generation shrine priest: the late Yamato Okano (brother of {{ill|Hirohiko Okano|ja|岡野弘彦|12=draft}}), who was born at this shrine. {{stub}} == Enshrined Deities == 【Main Deity】 • {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}} (Great Tsubame Deity) • {{ill|Empress Iwanohime|ja|磐之媛皇后|12=draft}} (Katsuragi Iwanohime) 【Secondary Deities】 • {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子|12=draft}} and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女|12=draft}} • Hachinaga-hime • Katsuragi Osatsuhiko • {{ill|Takeuchi no Sukune|ja|武内宿禰|12=draft}}-no-mikoto • Yorihime Ogara Sukune-no-mikoto 【Co-Enshrinement】 • Second Hall: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=draft}}; with secondary enshrinement of {{ill|Amaterasu Omikami|ja|大日孁貴神|12=draft}} • Third Hall: {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}} and the Hachiman Deity ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|12=draft}} and his aspect {{ill|Hota Wake|ja|誉田別尊|12=draft}}) 【Subsidiary Shrines (Sessha)】 • Takamiya: {{ill|Ise Ōkunidama-no-Okami|ja|伊勢大国魂大神|12=draft}} (Ise Tsuhiko-no-Kami) • Branch Shrines:  – Kitofusha: honoring figures such as Kitabatake Akeno-kō, Tōdō Kōji-kō, and Inari  – Michibiki Inari: associated with successive generations of the Osaki family (including the lineage of the current shrine head’s maternal Kubokou family and, ultimately, the last head priest of Kyoto Yasaka Shrine)  – Sakayakakino Yama-no-Kami, Misaka Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Hiurasaka); Sano-ya Yama-no-Kami (originally from Nakano); Tetsurakae Yama-no-Kami (originally from Aichi); and Kanayama Hiko-no-Mikoto • Fudansha: enshrining Tentsuibun Ōkami, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾尊|12=draft}}, and the purification waterfall (the source of Unidegawa); also Tsukuyomi-sha, enshrining the deity Tsukuyomi • Fudōsha: honoring {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇大神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津比売神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hayaakitsuhiko/Hayaakitsuhime|ja|速開都比売神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kibikado Shukunokami|ja|気吹戸主神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Haya-sasurahime|ja|速佐須良比売神|12=draft}}, along with Izanami-no-Mikoto and the Harai Waterfall (also known as Utoru-taki or the Couple’s Waterfall); additionally, Fudōson as offered by Katsura in dedication • Komiyasha: enshrining Gofuku Hime-no-Mikoto, among others; and Fujishinroku-sha, honoring Konohanasa Kuya Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Aichi) • Tateishisha: enshrining {{ill|Yamimie-no-Kami|ja|闇龗神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Takakagami|ja|高龗神|12=draft}} • Manorikisha: enshrining {{ill|Tenmoku Ikka-no-Mikoto|ja|天目一箇命|12=draft}} • Akagoishasha: enshrining {{ill|Nakisawa Megami|ja|泣沢女神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Iwanagahime|ja|磐長姫尊|12=draft}} == Main Treasures == The lantern stone associated with the tradition of Japanese filial piety (the “Twenty-Four Filial Exemplars”)—attributed to the tutelary deity Yamamoto Mokubei—is located in Nakamura Kurihara. The related lantern, once positioned before the Ota-ya, now stands in front of the Nino Torii parking lot, and the gourd field bestowed by the Tōdō domain lord is situated in Sakamoto. Also featured are the guardian dogs of the Komiyasha and those of the former Kitofusha (which enshrined deities such as {{ill|Kitabatake Akeno|ja|北畠顕能|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tōdō Kōji|ja|藤堂高次|12=draft}}). == External Links == * {{Official website|name=Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine Official Website|https://www.kawakamisanwakamiyahachiman.jp|format=flash}} * [https://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2929.html Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜Tourist Spot｜Tourism Mie] * [https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/918/ Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜See & Experience｜Official Tsu City Tourism Association website ...] {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tsu City]] [[Category:Buildings in Tsu City]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Iwanaga Faith]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:川上山若宮八幡宮]]

{{Multiple Issues |Source citation = March 2024 |Original research = March 2024 |No equivocation = March 2024 |Wikify = March 2024 |Excessive content = March 2024 }} {{Shrine | Name = 川上山若宮八幡宮 | Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}} {{ill|Tsu City|ja|津市|12=draft}} Misugi Town, Kawakami 3498 | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|27|49.3|N|136|15|57.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}}　{{ill|Empress Iwanohime|ja|磐之媛皇后|12=draft}} | Founded = Emperor Richū's era (400–406 AD) | Rank = Unranked shrine; the main hall, being a shared shrine, is classified as a village shrine | Annual Festival = October 19 | Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri | image = [[画像:川上山若宮八幡宮.JPG|200px]]
拝殿 | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mie Prefecture|label=川上山若宮八幡宮|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine''' (川上山若宮八幡宮) is a {{ill|Wakamiyahachiman Shrine|ja|若宮八幡宮|12=draft}} located in Misugi Town, {{ill|Tsu City|ja|津市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}. Its nicknames include “Kawakami-sama”, “Wakamiya-sama”, “Kawakami Hachiman-sama”, and “Wakamiya Hachiman-sama” among others. It is the oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine in Japan. == Shrine Legend == According to the shrine legend, in the fief of Empress Iwanohime (磐之媛皇后) in Iwano-go, Emperor {{ill|Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}} carried out the development of the region. In the era of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇|12=draft}} (early 5th century AD), the local residents, in commemoration of the virtues of Emperor Nintoku and Empress Iwanohime, chose this pure land of Kawakami at the foot of a sacred shugendō mountain overlooking the {{ill|Ise Plain|ja|伊勢平野|12=draft}} to establish the shrine and enshrine their revered spirit, thereby creating Japan’s oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. The shrine was highly revered by the Ise provincial governor’s family, the {{ill|Kitabatake family|ja|北畠家|12=draft}}. Out of such veneration, a castle was built in the neighboring village of Taki, and after donating sacred lands, the elder of the Toriya-o family was appointed as a permanent magistrate. The venerable Sasabe Kanta Yuka of the Okano main family was designated as the hereditary shrine priest with the newly conferred title of “Taiyū.” Initially, there were twelve Taiyū, and during the Tōdō domain period, there were eight. The Kanta Yuka shrine priest, entrusted with esoteric (yin-yang) management, held the authority to appoint and dismiss sumo-style guardians such as Inosuke and his counterparts. Furthermore, it is said that in order to preserve the shrine’s dignity, the Kitabatake clan installed barriers beyond the Okano priest family residence to prohibit general public worship. During the grand spring and autumn festivals, the Ise provincial governor himself would visit the shrine, and new policies were always implemented in accordance with divine will. Prior to the Edo period, in the era of shinbutsu shūgō (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine possessed many affiliated temples. Descendants of {{ill|Hira Sosei|ja|平宗清|12=draft}}—namely, the Hioki, Fukuchi, and the three factions of Iga Tsuge Uenokata (from the Kitamura group)—revered the shrine as their tutelary deity. It is also a shrine of monthly pilgrimage, prayer, and vow fulfillment (including vows for vengeance) associated with a renowned Daoist master from Ichinoi of {{ill|Iga|ja|伊賀市|12=draft}} (also linked with {{ill|Nabari City|ja|名張市|12=draft}}), known as Kotarō (Hachiman) Chōja and his son. The lords of the Tōdō domain also regarded it as a prayer site and invariably visited during the grand spring and autumn festivals to offer their votive contributions. Figures such as {{ill|En no Ozunu|ja|役小角|12=draft}}, Otani Yoshitsugu (大谷吉継), and the master swordsman of the {{ill|Kashima Shintō-ryū|ja|鹿島新當流|12=draft}}, Tsukahara Bokuden, are said to have developed the secret kusarigama technique here. In the precincts, the shrine priest known as Kanta Yuka is also reputed to have transmitted the first sword to {{ill|Kitabatake Tomonori|ja|北畠具教|12=draft}}. Moreover, renowned ascetics including Inryū Aisū Ikōsai ({{ill|Aisū Hisatada|ja|愛洲久忠|12=draft}}), Yagyū Shigenaga, and Shinkage-ryū Yagyū Ishūsai ({{ill|Yagyū Munenori|ja|柳生宗厳|12=draft}}) have visited this sacred site for their waterfall ascetic practices. Since the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, during the medieval era the shrine flourished as a central sacred site for shugendō in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}. Furthermore, the origins of the shrine trace back even further—to an era when the sacred shugendō mountains (beginning with Shugyengyō-san), Mount Hiraura, Mount Kyokuzan (the “Western Peak”), Mount Takami (the “Eastern Peak”), and the three peaks of the Kawakami Thirteen Peaks of {{ill|Hakusan|ja|白山|12=draft}} (三畝) along with Gakunō-dō Mountain, Mount Karatani (洞谷山), Mount Kuroiwa, Mount Gomazan, Mount Kosumayama, Mount Kōren, Mount Butsurai, and Mount Hakuran—served as shintai (sacred mountains) with many stone altars scattered throughout, giving rise to widespread ancestral spirit worship in the regions of Ise, Iga, Yamato, and Kii. Within the shrine grounds, the Tengu Rock is said to be the site where {{ill|Emperor Hanayama|ja|花山天皇|12=draft}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇|12=draft}} practiced the Great Tengu Method in their aspiration to become Japan’s great tengu. == Alternate Shrine Legend 1 == According to an alternate legend, Ennyōbō Chintoku Shōnin (the shrine priest of this shrine and concurrently the priest of the Kitabatake Castle Hachiman Shrine) received a prophetic dream warning of an imminent conflagration. He learned that from Kaga Hakusan a total of eight shrines were to be established in Isshigun—seven associated with Kaga Hakusan and one including the shrine’s own sacred mountain. On the night of January 24 in the 22nd year of Tenmon (1553), he experienced a divine message in his dream: “By next February, Kaga Hakusan will be consumed by fire, so I must relocate to the land where Amaterasu resides before then.” He also observed the Big Dipper scattered across the southern sky. Though he wished to set out for Kaga immediately, the heavy winter in Hokuriku forced him to wait until the snow subsided. In early March, he visited Hakusan and engaged in a seven-day, seven-night prayer. On the seventh night, a divine message came; the following morning, he found seven gohei (sacred wand offerings) standing upright inside his travel case. Taking this as confirmation of his prophetic dream, he prostrated himself before the deity and set off home with his travel case on his back. While pausing in his hometown to visit his elderly mother, he rested his travel case on a roadside stone along the bank of {{ill|Unidegawa|ja|雲出川|12=draft}} at Seto-ga-fuchi near his family castle. Suddenly, the case began to sway, and from it, seven white herons took flight. These herons were regarded as divine messengers. The number seven was seen as echoing the Big Dipper he had seen earlier. Moved by this sign, he resolved to build a Hakusan Shrine at the place where the seven white herons alighted. They descended at seven locations—the Forest of the Small Wa Wind God, the boundary at Iugawaguchi, Hatayano, Yamadano, the family castle, Takehara, and Iifukuda—and he simultaneously initiated the construction of shrine buildings at these sites. It is said that he then enshrined a portion of the spirit of Kaga Hakusan. This is known as the Legend of the Three White Heron Deities. == Alternate Shrine Legend 2 == Another tradition relates that the severed heads of {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子|12=draft}}—who defied Emperor Nintoku and fled the capital in pursuit of Ise—and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女|12=draft}}, were carried by retainers and enshrined here. According to this legend, their heads washed ashore at various locations such as Kawagami in Hakusan Town at Kawaguchi Seto, Hatayano in Hakusan Town, Yamato Soni Village, and Takehara Nakano in Misugi Town. Additionally, within the Fujiwara Chikakata legend—linked with Iga Kawakami (in Aoyama Town) and Mammi in Hakusan Town—a severed head is said to have floated upriver and drifted to the Kawakami area, borne by the Four Demons. These traditions reveal the complex, ancestral spirit–worship facet of the Wakamiyahachiman belief. Along the route, the {{ill|Ise Ondo|ja|伊勢音頭|12=draft}} is sung with the words that if one offers a heartfelt wish to Kawakami-sama (Wakamiya-sama), even if it seems a little beyond reach, it will be granted. It is said that not only in Ise but also in Shima, Owari, Mikawa, Mino, Hida, Tōtōmi, Izu, Yamato, Ōmi, Echigo, Etchu, Echizen, Kii, and Rakuchū did the divine “sword edge” descend—bestowing blessings from both Ise and this shrine. == Ise Tea Origin Site, Fuji‑kō, and Flood Control == Kawakami is also famed as the origin of Ise tea, for along the shrine’s approach lies the site of the first tea grove (Chakubo) planted by the priest {{ill|Myōe|ja|明恵|12=draft}} (also known as Kōben). The shrine priest family’s tea garden has existed since that time. In addition, the birthplace of the sixth-generation Fuji‑kō, {{ill|Shokugyōshinroku|ja|食行身禄|12=draft}}, is located at the Shimizui in the parish area, and there is also the record of the previous monk Shōkō Nyūtei. Since the Edo period it has been said that three entities surpass Isshigun in renown: Shokugyōshinroku, Matsuura Takeshirō (松浦武四郎), and Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. • Saijima Hachibei; the Unide Irrigation Canal (in the Kōchaya area, where a branch shrine dedicated to the shrine’s spirit water exists); Manno Pond (founded by Prince Kōbō Daishi and Kūkai, later restored by the Hosokawa family). • Yamanaka Tametsuna; the Kōya Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Kōya, Isshigun); Matsui Magoemon; the Ise Miyagawa Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Miyagawa Town, Ise City); Tendai Shinseisō Shōnin; and others who played significant roles in flood control projects (in imitation of the legend of Emperor Nintoku)—a fact that accounts for the many branch shrines dedicated to flood control deities in Owari, Mino, Iga, and Mikawa. == Return Stone == If one applies at the shrine office, prayers for the fulfillment of heartfelt wishes, longevity, good fortune, well-being of wells, and assured victory over one’s enemies are offered. In return, “sacred stones” are bestowed for enshrinement in household altars and wells, and upon the fulfillment of the prayer, a ritual is performed in which the number of stones is doubled—this “Return Stone” ritual has been carried out since ancient times. == Related Persons == 34th-generation shrine priest: the late Hirokatsu Okano, the eldest son of his wife Kei. 35th-generation shrine priest: the late Yamato Okano (brother of {{ill|Hirohiko Okano|ja|岡野弘彦|12=draft}}), who was born at this shrine. {{stub}} == Enshrined Deities == 【Main Deity】 • {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}} (Great Tsubame Deity) • {{ill|Empress Iwanohime|ja|磐之媛皇后|12=draft}} (Katsuragi Iwanohime) 【Secondary Deities】 • {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子|12=draft}} and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女|12=draft}} • Hachinaga-hime • Katsuragi Osatsuhiko • {{ill|Takeuchi no Sukune|ja|武内宿禰|12=draft}}-no-mikoto • Yorihime Ogara Sukune-no-mikoto 【Co-Enshrinement】 • Second Hall: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=draft}}; with secondary enshrinement of {{ill|Amaterasu Omikami|ja|大日孁貴神|12=draft}} • Third Hall: {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}} and the Hachiman Deity ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|12=draft}} and his aspect {{ill|Hota Wake|ja|誉田別尊|12=draft}}) 【Subsidiary Shrines (Sessha)】 • Takamiya: {{ill|Ise Ōkunidama-no-Okami|ja|伊勢大国魂大神|12=draft}} (Ise Tsuhiko-no-Kami) • Branch Shrines:  – Kitofusha: honoring figures such as Kitabatake Akeno-kō, Tōdō Kōji-kō, and Inari  – Michibiki Inari: associated with successive generations of the Osaki family (including the lineage of the current shrine head’s maternal Kubokou family and, ultimately, the last head priest of Kyoto Yasaka Shrine)  – Sakayakakino Yama-no-Kami, Misaka Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Hiurasaka); Sano-ya Yama-no-Kami (originally from Nakano); Tetsurakae Yama-no-Kami (originally from Aichi); and Kanayama Hiko-no-Mikoto • Fudansha: enshrining Tentsuibun Ōkami, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾尊|12=draft}}, and the purification waterfall (the source of Unidegawa); also Tsukuyomi-sha, enshrining the deity Tsukuyomi • Fudōsha: honoring {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇大神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津比売神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hayaakitsuhiko/Hayaakitsuhime|ja|速開都比売神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kibikado Shukunokami|ja|気吹戸主神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Haya-sasurahime|ja|速佐須良比売神|12=draft}}, along with Izanami-no-Mikoto and the Harai Waterfall (also known as Utoru-taki or the Couple’s Waterfall); additionally, Fudōson as offered by Katsura in dedication • Komiyasha: enshrining Gofuku Hime-no-Mikoto, among others; and Fujishinroku-sha, honoring Konohanasa Kuya Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Aichi) • Tateishisha: enshrining {{ill|Yamimie-no-Kami|ja|闇龗神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Takakagami|ja|高龗神|12=draft}} • Manorikisha: enshrining {{ill|Tenmoku Ikka-no-Mikoto|ja|天目一箇命|12=draft}} • Akagoishasha: enshrining {{ill|Nakisawa Megami|ja|泣沢女神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Iwanagahime|ja|磐長姫尊|12=draft}} == Main Treasures == The lantern stone associated with the tradition of Japanese filial piety (the “Twenty-Four Filial Exemplars”)—attributed to the tutelary deity Yamamoto Mokubei—is located in Nakamura Kurihara. The related lantern, once positioned before the Ota-ya, now stands in front of the Nino Torii parking lot, and the gourd field bestowed by the Tōdō domain lord is situated in Sakamoto. Also featured are the guardian dogs of the Komiyasha and those of the former Kitofusha (which enshrined deities such as {{ill|Kitabatake Akeno|ja|北畠顕能|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tōdō Kōji|ja|藤堂高次|12=draft}}). == External Links == * {{Official website|name=Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine Official Website|https://www.kawakamisanwakamiyahachiman.jp|format=flash}} * [https://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2929.html Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜Tourist Spot｜Tourism Mie] * [https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/918/ Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜See & Experience｜Official Tsu City Tourism Association website ...] {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tsu City]] [[Category:Buildings in Tsu City]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Iwanaga Faith]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:川上山若宮八幡宮]]

拝殿 | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mie Prefecture|label=川上山若宮八幡宮|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine''' (川上山若宮八幡宮) is a {{ill|Wakamiyahachiman Shrine|ja|若宮八幡宮|12=draft}} located in Misugi Town, {{ill|Tsu City|ja|津市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}. Its nicknames include “Kawakami-sama”, “Wakamiya-sama”, “Kawakami Hachiman-sama”, and “Wakamiya Hachiman-sama” among others. It is the oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine in Japan. == Shrine Legend == According to the shrine legend, in the fief of Empress Iwanohime (磐之媛皇后) in Iwano-go, Emperor {{ill|Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}} carried out the development of the region. In the era of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇|12=draft}} (early 5th century AD), the local residents, in commemoration of the virtues of Emperor Nintoku and Empress Iwanohime, chose this pure land of Kawakami at the foot of a sacred shugendō mountain overlooking the {{ill|Ise Plain|ja|伊勢平野|12=draft}} to establish the shrine and enshrine their revered spirit, thereby creating Japan’s oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. The shrine was highly revered by the Ise provincial governor’s family, the {{ill|Kitabatake family|ja|北畠家|12=draft}}. Out of such veneration, a castle was built in the neighboring village of Taki, and after donating sacred lands, the elder of the Toriya-o family was appointed as a permanent magistrate. The venerable Sasabe Kanta Yuka of the Okano main family was designated as the hereditary shrine priest with the newly conferred title of “Taiyū.” Initially, there were twelve Taiyū, and during the Tōdō domain period, there were eight. The Kanta Yuka shrine priest, entrusted with esoteric (yin-yang) management, held the authority to appoint and dismiss sumo-style guardians such as Inosuke and his counterparts. Furthermore, it is said that in order to preserve the shrine’s dignity, the Kitabatake clan installed barriers beyond the Okano priest family residence to prohibit general public worship. During the grand spring and autumn festivals, the Ise provincial governor himself would visit the shrine, and new policies were always implemented in accordance with divine will. Prior to the Edo period, in the era of shinbutsu shūgō (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine possessed many affiliated temples. Descendants of {{ill|Hira Sosei|ja|平宗清|12=draft}}—namely, the Hioki, Fukuchi, and the three factions of Iga Tsuge Uenokata (from the Kitamura group)—revered the shrine as their tutelary deity. It is also a shrine of monthly pilgrimage, prayer, and vow fulfillment (including vows for vengeance) associated with a renowned Daoist master from Ichinoi of {{ill|Iga|ja|伊賀市|12=draft}} (also linked with {{ill|Nabari City|ja|名張市|12=draft}}), known as Kotarō (Hachiman) Chōja and his son. The lords of the Tōdō domain also regarded it as a prayer site and invariably visited during the grand spring and autumn festivals to offer their votive contributions. Figures such as {{ill|En no Ozunu|ja|役小角|12=draft}}, Otani Yoshitsugu (大谷吉継), and the master swordsman of the {{ill|Kashima Shintō-ryū|ja|鹿島新當流|12=draft}}, Tsukahara Bokuden, are said to have developed the secret kusarigama technique here. In the precincts, the shrine priest known as Kanta Yuka is also reputed to have transmitted the first sword to {{ill|Kitabatake Tomonori|ja|北畠具教|12=draft}}. Moreover, renowned ascetics including Inryū Aisū Ikōsai ({{ill|Aisū Hisatada|ja|愛洲久忠|12=draft}}), Yagyū Shigenaga, and Shinkage-ryū Yagyū Ishūsai ({{ill|Yagyū Munenori|ja|柳生宗厳|12=draft}}) have visited this sacred site for their waterfall ascetic practices. Since the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, during the medieval era the shrine flourished as a central sacred site for shugendō in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}. Furthermore, the origins of the shrine trace back even further—to an era when the sacred shugendō mountains (beginning with Shugyengyō-san), Mount Hiraura, Mount Kyokuzan (the “Western Peak”), Mount Takami (the “Eastern Peak”), and the three peaks of the Kawakami Thirteen Peaks of {{ill|Hakusan|ja|白山|12=draft}} (三畝) along with Gakunō-dō Mountain, Mount Karatani (洞谷山), Mount Kuroiwa, Mount Gomazan, Mount Kosumayama, Mount Kōren, Mount Butsurai, and Mount Hakuran—served as shintai (sacred mountains) with many stone altars scattered throughout, giving rise to widespread ancestral spirit worship in the regions of Ise, Iga, Yamato, and Kii. Within the shrine grounds, the Tengu Rock is said to be the site where {{ill|Emperor Hanayama|ja|花山天皇|12=draft}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇|12=draft}} practiced the Great Tengu Method in their aspiration to become Japan’s great tengu. == Alternate Shrine Legend 1 == According to an alternate legend, Ennyōbō Chintoku Shōnin (the shrine priest of this shrine and concurrently the priest of the Kitabatake Castle Hachiman Shrine) received a prophetic dream warning of an imminent conflagration. He learned that from Kaga Hakusan a total of eight shrines were to be established in Isshigun—seven associated with Kaga Hakusan and one including the shrine’s own sacred mountain. On the night of January 24 in the 22nd year of Tenmon (1553), he experienced a divine message in his dream: “By next February, Kaga Hakusan will be consumed by fire, so I must relocate to the land where Amaterasu resides before then.” He also observed the Big Dipper scattered across the southern sky. Though he wished to set out for Kaga immediately, the heavy winter in Hokuriku forced him to wait until the snow subsided. In early March, he visited Hakusan and engaged in a seven-day, seven-night prayer. On the seventh night, a divine message came; the following morning, he found seven gohei (sacred wand offerings) standing upright inside his travel case. Taking this as confirmation of his prophetic dream, he prostrated himself before the deity and set off home with his travel case on his back. While pausing in his hometown to visit his elderly mother, he rested his travel case on a roadside stone along the bank of {{ill|Unidegawa|ja|雲出川|12=draft}} at Seto-ga-fuchi near his family castle. Suddenly, the case began to sway, and from it, seven white herons took flight. These herons were regarded as divine messengers. The number seven was seen as echoing the Big Dipper he had seen earlier. Moved by this sign, he resolved to build a Hakusan Shrine at the place where the seven white herons alighted. They descended at seven locations—the Forest of the Small Wa Wind God, the boundary at Iugawaguchi, Hatayano, Yamadano, the family castle, Takehara, and Iifukuda—and he simultaneously initiated the construction of shrine buildings at these sites. It is said that he then enshrined a portion of the spirit of Kaga Hakusan. This is known as the Legend of the Three White Heron Deities. == Alternate Shrine Legend 2 == Another tradition relates that the severed heads of {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子|12=draft}}—who defied Emperor Nintoku and fled the capital in pursuit of Ise—and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女|12=draft}}, were carried by retainers and enshrined here. According to this legend, their heads washed ashore at various locations such as Kawagami in Hakusan Town at Kawaguchi Seto, Hatayano in Hakusan Town, Yamato Soni Village, and Takehara Nakano in Misugi Town. Additionally, within the Fujiwara Chikakata legend—linked with Iga Kawakami (in Aoyama Town) and Mammi in Hakusan Town—a severed head is said to have floated upriver and drifted to the Kawakami area, borne by the Four Demons. These traditions reveal the complex, ancestral spirit–worship facet of the Wakamiyahachiman belief. Along the route, the {{ill|Ise Ondo|ja|伊勢音頭|12=draft}} is sung with the words that if one offers a heartfelt wish to Kawakami-sama (Wakamiya-sama), even if it seems a little beyond reach, it will be granted. It is said that not only in Ise but also in Shima, Owari, Mikawa, Mino, Hida, Tōtōmi, Izu, Yamato, Ōmi, Echigo, Etchu, Echizen, Kii, and Rakuchū did the divine “sword edge” descend—bestowing blessings from both Ise and this shrine. == Ise Tea Origin Site, Fuji‑kō, and Flood Control == Kawakami is also famed as the origin of Ise tea, for along the shrine’s approach lies the site of the first tea grove (Chakubo) planted by the priest {{ill|Myōe|ja|明恵|12=draft}} (also known as Kōben). The shrine priest family’s tea garden has existed since that time. In addition, the birthplace of the sixth-generation Fuji‑kō, {{ill|Shokugyōshinroku|ja|食行身禄|12=draft}}, is located at the Shimizui in the parish area, and there is also the record of the previous monk Shōkō Nyūtei. Since the Edo period it has been said that three entities surpass Isshigun in renown: Shokugyōshinroku, Matsuura Takeshirō (松浦武四郎), and Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. • Saijima Hachibei; the Unide Irrigation Canal (in the Kōchaya area, where a branch shrine dedicated to the shrine’s spirit water exists); Manno Pond (founded by Prince Kōbō Daishi and Kūkai, later restored by the Hosokawa family). • Yamanaka Tametsuna; the Kōya Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Kōya, Isshigun); Matsui Magoemon; the Ise Miyagawa Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Miyagawa Town, Ise City); Tendai Shinseisō Shōnin; and others who played significant roles in flood control projects (in imitation of the legend of Emperor Nintoku)—a fact that accounts for the many branch shrines dedicated to flood control deities in Owari, Mino, Iga, and Mikawa. == Return Stone == If one applies at the shrine office, prayers for the fulfillment of heartfelt wishes, longevity, good fortune, well-being of wells, and assured victory over one’s enemies are offered. In return, “sacred stones” are bestowed for enshrinement in household altars and wells, and upon the fulfillment of the prayer, a ritual is performed in which the number of stones is doubled—this “Return Stone” ritual has been carried out since ancient times. == Related Persons == 34th-generation shrine priest: the late Hirokatsu Okano, the eldest son of his wife Kei. 35th-generation shrine priest: the late Yamato Okano (brother of {{ill|Hirohiko Okano|ja|岡野弘彦|12=draft}}), who was born at this shrine. {{stub}} == Enshrined Deities == 【Main Deity】 • {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}} (Great Tsubame Deity) • {{ill|Empress Iwanohime|ja|磐之媛皇后|12=draft}} (Katsuragi Iwanohime) 【Secondary Deities】 • {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子|12=draft}} and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女|12=draft}} • Hachinaga-hime • Katsuragi Osatsuhiko • {{ill|Takeuchi no Sukune|ja|武内宿禰|12=draft}}-no-mikoto • Yorihime Ogara Sukune-no-mikoto 【Co-Enshrinement】 • Second Hall: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=draft}}; with secondary enshrinement of {{ill|Amaterasu Omikami|ja|大日孁貴神|12=draft}} • Third Hall: {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}} and the Hachiman Deity ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|12=draft}} and his aspect {{ill|Hota Wake|ja|誉田別尊|12=draft}}) 【Subsidiary Shrines (Sessha)】 • Takamiya: {{ill|Ise Ōkunidama-no-Okami|ja|伊勢大国魂大神|12=draft}} (Ise Tsuhiko-no-Kami) • Branch Shrines:  – Kitofusha: honoring figures such as Kitabatake Akeno-kō, Tōdō Kōji-kō, and Inari  – Michibiki Inari: associated with successive generations of the Osaki family (including the lineage of the current shrine head’s maternal Kubokou family and, ultimately, the last head priest of Kyoto Yasaka Shrine)  – Sakayakakino Yama-no-Kami, Misaka Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Hiurasaka); Sano-ya Yama-no-Kami (originally from Nakano); Tetsurakae Yama-no-Kami (originally from Aichi); and Kanayama Hiko-no-Mikoto • Fudansha: enshrining Tentsuibun Ōkami, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾尊|12=draft}}, and the purification waterfall (the source of Unidegawa); also Tsukuyomi-sha, enshrining the deity Tsukuyomi • Fudōsha: honoring {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇大神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津比売神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hayaakitsuhiko/Hayaakitsuhime|ja|速開都比売神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kibikado Shukunokami|ja|気吹戸主神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Haya-sasurahime|ja|速佐須良比売神|12=draft}}, along with Izanami-no-Mikoto and the Harai Waterfall (also known as Utoru-taki or the Couple’s Waterfall); additionally, Fudōson as offered by Katsura in dedication • Komiyasha: enshrining Gofuku Hime-no-Mikoto, among others; and Fujishinroku-sha, honoring Konohanasa Kuya Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Aichi) • Tateishisha: enshrining {{ill|Yamimie-no-Kami|ja|闇龗神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Takakagami|ja|高龗神|12=draft}} • Manorikisha: enshrining {{ill|Tenmoku Ikka-no-Mikoto|ja|天目一箇命|12=draft}} • Akagoishasha: enshrining {{ill|Nakisawa Megami|ja|泣沢女神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Iwanagahime|ja|磐長姫尊|12=draft}} == Main Treasures == The lantern stone associated with the tradition of Japanese filial piety (the “Twenty-Four Filial Exemplars”)—attributed to the tutelary deity Yamamoto Mokubei—is located in Nakamura Kurihara. The related lantern, once positioned before the Ota-ya, now stands in front of the Nino Torii parking lot, and the gourd field bestowed by the Tōdō domain lord is situated in Sakamoto. Also featured are the guardian dogs of the Komiyasha and those of the former Kitofusha (which enshrined deities such as {{ill|Kitabatake Akeno|ja|北畠顕能|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tōdō Kōji|ja|藤堂高次|12=draft}}). == External Links == * {{Official website|name=Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine Official Website|https://www.kawakamisanwakamiyahachiman.jp|format=flash}} * [https://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2929.html Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜Tourist Spot｜Tourism Mie] * [https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/918/ Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜See & Experience｜Official Tsu City Tourism Association website ...] {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tsu City]] [[Category:Buildings in Tsu City]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Iwanaga Faith]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:川上山若宮八幡宮]]

{{Multiple Issues |Source citation = March 2024 |Original research = March 2024 |No equivocation = March 2024 |Wikify = March 2024 |Excessive content = March 2024 }} {{Shrine | Name = 川上山若宮八幡宮 | Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}} {{ill|Tsu City|ja|津市|12=draft}} Misugi Town, Kawakami 3498 | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|27|49.3|N|136|15|57.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}}　{{ill|Empress Iwanohime|ja|磐之媛皇后|12=draft}} | Founded = Emperor Richū's era (400–406 AD) | Rank = UnRanked shrine; the main hall, being a shared shrine, is classified as a village shrine | Annual Festival = October 19 | Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri | image = [[画像:川上山若宮八幡宮.JPG|200px]]
拝殿 | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mie Prefecture|label=川上山若宮八幡宮|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine''' (川上山若宮八幡宮) is a {{ill|Wakamiyahachiman Shrine|ja|若宮八幡宮|12=draft}} located in Misugi Town, {{ill|Tsu City|ja|津市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}. Its nicknames include “Kawakami-sama”, “Wakamiya-sama”, “Kawakami Hachiman-sama”, and “Wakamiya Hachiman-sama” among others. It is the oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine in Japan. == Shrine Legend == According to the shrine legend, in the fief of Empress Iwanohime (磐之媛皇后) in Iwano-go, Emperor {{ill|Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}} carried out the development of the region. In the era of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇|12=draft}} (early 5th century AD), the local residents, in commemoration of the virtues of Emperor Nintoku and Empress Iwanohime, chose this pure land of Kawakami at the foot of a sacred shugendō mountain overlooking the {{ill|Ise Plain|ja|伊勢平野|12=draft}} to establish the shrine and enshrine their revered spirit, thereby creating Japan’s oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. The shrine was highly revered by the Ise provincial governor’s family, the {{ill|Kitabatake family|ja|北畠家|12=draft}}. Out of such veneration, a castle was built in the neighboring village of Taki, and after donating sacred lands, the elder of the Toriya-o family was appointed as a permanent magistrate. The venerable Sasabe Kanta Yuka of the Okano main family was designated as the hereditary shrine priest with the newly conferred title of “Taiyū.” Initially, there were twelve Taiyū, and during the Tōdō domain period, there were eight. The Kanta Yuka shrine priest, entrusted with esoteric (yin-yang) management, held the authority to appoint and dismiss sumo-style guardians such as Inosuke and his counterparts. Furthermore, it is said that in order to preserve the shrine’s dignity, the Kitabatake clan installed barriers beyond the Okano priest family residence to prohibit general public worship. During the grand spring and autumn festivals, the Ise provincial governor himself would visit the shrine, and new policies were always implemented in accordance with divine will. Prior to the Edo period, in the era of shinbutsu shūgō (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine possessed many affiliated temples. Descendants of {{ill|Hira Sosei|ja|平宗清|12=draft}}—namely, the Hioki, Fukuchi, and the three factions of Iga Tsuge Uenokata (from the Kitamura group)—revered the shrine as their tutelary deity. It is also a shrine of monthly pilgrimage, prayer, and vow fulfillment (including vows for vengeance) associated with a renowned Daoist master from Ichinoi of {{ill|Iga|ja|伊賀市|12=draft}} (also linked with {{ill|Nabari City|ja|名張市|12=draft}}), known as Kotarō (Hachiman) Chōja and his son. The lords of the Tōdō domain also regarded it as a prayer site and invariably visited during the grand spring and autumn festivals to offer their votive contributions. Figures such as {{ill|En no Ozunu|ja|役小角|12=draft}}, Otani Yoshitsugu (大谷吉継), and the master swordsman of the {{ill|Kashima Shintō-ryū|ja|鹿島新當流|12=draft}}, Tsukahara Bokuden, are said to have developed the secret kusarigama technique here. In the precincts, the shrine priest known as Kanta Yuka is also reputed to have transmitted the first sword to {{ill|Kitabatake Tomonori|ja|北畠具教|12=draft}}. Moreover, renowned ascetics including Inryū Aisū Ikōsai ({{ill|Aisū Hisatada|ja|愛洲久忠|12=draft}}), Yagyū Shigenaga, and Shinkage-ryū Yagyū Ishūsai ({{ill|Yagyū Munenori|ja|柳生宗厳|12=draft}}) have visited this sacred site for their waterfall ascetic practices. Since the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, during the medieval era the shrine flourished as a central sacred site for shugendō in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}. Furthermore, the origins of the shrine trace back even further—to an era when the sacred shugendō mountains (beginning with Shugyengyō-san), Mount Hiraura, Mount Kyokuzan (the “Western Peak”), Mount Takami (the “Eastern Peak”), and the three peaks of the Kawakami Thirteen Peaks of {{ill|Hakusan|ja|白山|12=draft}} (三畝) along with Gakunō-dō Mountain, Mount Karatani (洞谷山), Mount Kuroiwa, Mount Gomazan, Mount Kosumayama, Mount Kōren, Mount Butsurai, and Mount Hakuran—served as shintai (sacred mountains) with many stone altars scattered throughout, giving rise to widespread ancestral spirit worship in the regions of Ise, Iga, Yamato, and Kii. Within the shrine grounds, the Tengu Rock is said to be the site where {{ill|Emperor Hanayama|ja|花山天皇|12=draft}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇|12=draft}} practiced the Great Tengu Method in their aspiration to become Japan’s great tengu. == Alternate Shrine Legend 1 == According to an alternate legend, Ennyōbō Chintoku Shōnin (the shrine priest of this shrine and concurrently the priest of the Kitabatake Castle Hachiman Shrine) received a prophetic dream warning of an imminent conflagration. He learned that from Kaga Hakusan a total of eight shrines were to be established in Isshigun—seven associated with Kaga Hakusan and one including the shrine’s own sacred mountain. On the night of January 24 in the 22nd year of Tenmon (1553), he experienced a divine message in his dream: “By next February, Kaga Hakusan will be consumed by fire, so I must relocate to the land where Amaterasu resides before then.” He also observed the Big Dipper scattered across the southern sky. Though he wished to set out for Kaga immediately, the heavy winter in Hokuriku forced him to wait until the snow subsided. In early March, he visited Hakusan and engaged in a seven-day, seven-night prayer. On the seventh night, a divine message came; the following morning, he found seven gohei (sacred wand offerings) standing upright inside his travel case. Taking this as confirmation of his prophetic dream, he prostrated himself before the deity and set off home with his travel case on his back. While pausing in his hometown to visit his elderly mother, he rested his travel case on a roadside stone along the bank of {{ill|Unidegawa|ja|雲出川|12=draft}} at Seto-ga-fuchi near his family castle. Suddenly, the case began to sway, and from it, seven white herons took flight. These herons were regarded as divine messengers. The number seven was seen as echoing the Big Dipper he had seen earlier. Moved by this sign, he resolved to build a Hakusan Shrine at the place where the seven white herons alighted. They descended at seven locations—the Forest of the Small Wa Wind God, the boundary at Iugawaguchi, Hatayano, Yamadano, the family castle, Takehara, and Iifukuda—and he simultaneously initiated the construction of shrine buildings at these sites. It is said that he then enshrined a portion of the spirit of Kaga Hakusan. This is known as the Legend of the Three White Heron Deities. == Alternate Shrine Legend 2 == Another tradition relates that the severed heads of {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子|12=draft}}—who defied Emperor Nintoku and fled the capital in pursuit of Ise—and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女|12=draft}}, were carried by retainers and enshrined here. According to this legend, their heads washed ashore at various locations such as Kawagami in Hakusan Town at Kawaguchi Seto, Hatayano in Hakusan Town, Yamato Soni Village, and Takehara Nakano in Misugi Town. Additionally, within the Fujiwara Chikakata legend—linked with Iga Kawakami (in Aoyama Town) and Mammi in Hakusan Town—a severed head is said to have floated upriver and drifted to the Kawakami area, borne by the Four Demons. These traditions reveal the complex, ancestral spirit–worship facet of the Wakamiyahachiman belief. Along the route, the {{ill|Ise Ondo|ja|伊勢音頭|12=draft}} is sung with the words that if one offers a heartfelt wish to Kawakami-sama (Wakamiya-sama), even if it seems a little beyond reach, it will be granted. It is said that not only in Ise but also in Shima, Owari, Mikawa, Mino, Hida, Tōtōmi, Izu, Yamato, Ōmi, Echigo, Etchu, Echizen, Kii, and Rakuchū did the divine “sword edge” descend—bestowing blessings from both Ise and this shrine. == Ise Tea Origin Site, Fuji‑kō, and Flood Control == Kawakami is also famed as the origin of Ise tea, for along the shrine’s approach lies the site of the first tea grove (Chakubo) planted by the priest {{ill|Myōe|ja|明恵|12=draft}} (also known as Kōben). The shrine priest family’s tea garden has existed since that time. In addition, the birthplace of the sixth-generation Fuji‑kō, {{ill|Shokugyōshinroku|ja|食行身禄|12=draft}}, is located at the Shimizui in the parish area, and there is also the record of the previous monk Shōkō Nyūtei. Since the Edo period it has been said that three entities surpass Isshigun in renown: Shokugyōshinroku, Matsuura Takeshirō (松浦武四郎), and Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. • Saijima Hachibei; the Unide Irrigation Canal (in the Kōchaya area, where a branch shrine dedicated to the shrine’s spirit water exists); Manno Pond (founded by Prince Kōbō Daishi and Kūkai, later restored by the Hosokawa family). • Yamanaka Tametsuna; the Kōya Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Kōya, Isshigun); Matsui Magoemon; the Ise Miyagawa Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Miyagawa Town, Ise City); Tendai Shinseisō Shōnin; and others who played significant roles in flood control projects (in imitation of the legend of Emperor Nintoku)—a fact that accounts for the many branch shrines dedicated to flood control deities in Owari, Mino, Iga, and Mikawa. == Return Stone == If one applies at the shrine office, prayers for the fulfillment of heartfelt wishes, longevity, good fortune, well-being of wells, and assured victory over one’s enemies are offered. In return, “sacred stones” are bestowed for enshrinement in household altars and wells, and upon the fulfillment of the prayer, a ritual is performed in which the number of stones is doubled—this “Return Stone” ritual has been carried out since ancient times. == Related Persons == 34th-generation shrine priest: the late Hirokatsu Okano, the eldest son of his wife Kei. 35th-generation shrine priest: the late Yamato Okano (brother of {{ill|Hirohiko Okano|ja|岡野弘彦|12=draft}}), who was born at this shrine. {{stub}} == Enshrined Deities == 【Main Deity】 • {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}} (Great Tsubame Deity) • {{ill|Empress Iwanohime|ja|磐之媛皇后|12=draft}} (Katsuragi Iwanohime) 【Secondary Deities】 • {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子|12=draft}} and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女|12=draft}} • Hachinaga-hime • Katsuragi Osatsuhiko • {{ill|Takeuchi no Sukune|ja|武内宿禰|12=draft}}-no-mikoto • Yorihime Ogara Sukune-no-mikoto 【Co-Enshrinement】 • Second Hall: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=draft}}; with secondary enshrinement of {{ill|Amaterasu Omikami|ja|大日孁貴神|12=draft}} • Third Hall: {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}} and the Hachiman Deity ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|12=draft}} and his aspect {{ill|Hota Wake|ja|誉田別尊|12=draft}}) 【Subsidiary Shrines (Sessha)】 • Takamiya: {{ill|Ise Ōkunidama-no-Okami|ja|伊勢大国魂大神|12=draft}} (Ise Tsuhiko-no-Kami) • Branch Shrines:  – Kitofusha: honoring figures such as Kitabatake Akeno-kō, Tōdō Kōji-kō, and Inari  – Michibiki Inari: associated with successive generations of the Osaki family (including the lineage of the current shrine head’s maternal Kubokou family and, ultimately, the last head priest of Kyoto Yasaka Shrine)  – Sakayakakino Yama-no-Kami, Misaka Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Hiurasaka); also Tsukuyomi-sha, enshrining the deity Tsukuyomi • Fudōsha: honoring {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇大神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津比売神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hayaakitsuhiko/Hayaakitsuhime|ja|速開都比売神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kibikado Shukunokami|ja|気吹戸主神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Haya-sasurahime|ja|速佐須良比売神|12=draft}}, along with Izanami-no-Mikoto and the Harai Waterfall (also known as Utoru-taki or the Couple’s Waterfall); additionally, Fudōson as offered by Katsura in dedication • Komiyasha: enshrining Gofuku Hime-no-Mikoto, among others; and Fujishinroku-sha, honoring Konohanasa Kuya Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Aichi) • Tateishisha: enshrining {{ill|Yamimie-no-Kami|ja|闇龗神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Takakagami|ja|高龗神|12=draft}} • Manorikisha: enshrining {{ill|Tenmoku Ikka-no-Mikoto|ja|天目一箇命|12=draft}} • Akagoishasha: enshrining {{ill|Nakisawa Megami|ja|泣沢女神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Iwanagahime|ja|磐長姫尊|12=draft}} == Main Treasures == The lantern stone associated with the tradition of Japanese filial piety (the “Twenty-Four Filial Exemplars”)—attributed to the tutelary deity Yamamoto Mokubei—is located in Nakamura Kurihara. The related lantern, once positioned before the Ota-ya, now stands in front of the Nino Torii parking lot, and the gourd field bestowed by the Tōdō domain lord is situated in Sakamoto. Also featured are the guardian dogs of the Komiyasha and those of the former Kitofusha (which enshrined deities such as {{ill|Kitabatake Akeno|ja|北畠顕能|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tōdō Kōji|ja|藤堂高次|12=draft}}). == External Links == * {{Official website|name=Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine Official Website|https://www.kawakamisanwakamiyahachiman.jp|format=flash}} * [https://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2929.html Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜Tourist Spot｜Tourism Mie] * [https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/918/ Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜See & Experience｜Official Tsu City Tourism Association website ...] {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tsu City]] [[Category:Buildings in Tsu City]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Iwanaga Faith]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:川上山若宮八幡宮]]

{{Multiple Issues |Source citation = March 2024 |Original research = March 2024 |No equivocation = March 2024 |Wikify = March 2024 |Excessive content = March 2024 }} {{Shrine | Name = 川上山若宮八幡宮 | Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} {{ill|Tsu City|ja|津市}} Misugi Town, Kawakami 3498 | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|27|49.3|N|136|15|57.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}　{{ill|Empress Iwanohime|ja|磐之媛皇后}} | Founded = Emperor Richū's era (400–406 AD) | Rank = UnRanked shrine; the main hall, being a shared shrine, is classified as a village shrine | Annual Festival = October 19 | Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri | image = [[画像:川上山若宮八幡宮.JPG|200px]]
拝殿 | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mie Prefecture|label=川上山若宮八幡宮|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine''' (川上山若宮八幡宮) is a {{ill|Wakamiyahachiman Shrine|ja|若宮八幡宮}} located in Misugi Town, {{ill|Tsu City|ja|津市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its nicknames include “Kawakami-sama”, “Wakamiya-sama”, “Kawakami Hachiman-sama”, and “Wakamiya Hachiman-sama” among others. It is the oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine in Japan. == Shrine Legend == According to the shrine legend, in the fief of Empress Iwanohime (磐之媛皇后) in Iwano-go, Emperor {{ill|Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} carried out the development of the region. In the era of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇}} (early 5th century AD), the local residents, in commemoration of the virtues of Emperor Nintoku and Empress Iwanohime, chose this pure land of Kawakami at the foot of a sacred shugendō mountain overlooking the {{ill|Ise Plain|ja|伊勢平野}} to establish the shrine and enshrine their revered spirit, thereby creating Japan’s oldest Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. The shrine was highly revered by the Ise provincial governor’s family, the {{ill|Kitabatake family|ja|北畠家}}. Out of such veneration, a castle was built in the neighboring village of Taki, and after donating sacred lands, the elder of the Toriya-o family was appointed as a permanent magistrate. The venerable Sasabe Kanta Yuka of the Okano main family was designated as the hereditary shrine priest with the newly conferred title of “Taiyū.” Initially, there were twelve Taiyū, and during the Tōdō domain period, there were eight. The Kanta Yuka shrine priest, entrusted with esoteric (yin-yang) management, held the authority to appoint and dismiss sumo-style guardians such as Inosuke and his counterparts. Furthermore, it is said that in order to preserve the shrine’s dignity, the Kitabatake clan installed barriers beyond the Okano priest family residence to prohibit general public worship. During the grand spring and autumn festivals, the Ise provincial governor himself would visit the shrine, and new policies were always implemented in accordance with divine will. Prior to the Edo period, in the era of shinbutsu shūgō (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine possessed many affiliated temples. Descendants of {{ill|Hira Sosei|ja|平宗清}}—namely, the Hioki, Fukuchi, and the three factions of Iga Tsuge Uenokata (from the Kitamura group)—revered the shrine as their tutelary deity. It is also a shrine of monthly pilgrimage, prayer, and vow fulfillment (including vows for vengeance) associated with a renowned Daoist master from Ichinoi of {{ill|Iga|ja|伊賀市}} (also linked with {{ill|Nabari City|ja|名張市}}), known as Kotarō (Hachiman) Chōja and his son. The lords of the Tōdō domain also regarded it as a prayer site and invariably visited during the grand spring and autumn festivals to offer their votive contributions. Figures such as {{ill|En no Ozunu|ja|役小角}}, Otani Yoshitsugu (大谷吉継), and the master swordsman of the {{ill|Kashima Shintō-ryū|ja|鹿島新當流}}, Tsukahara Bokuden, are said to have developed the secret kusarigama technique here. In the precincts, the shrine priest known as Kanta Yuka is also reputed to have transmitted the first sword to {{ill|Kitabatake Tomonori|ja|北畠具教}}. Moreover, renowned ascetics including Inryū Aisū Ikōsai ({{ill|Aisū Hisatada|ja|愛洲久忠}}), Yagyū Shigenaga, and Shinkage-ryū Yagyū Ishūsai ({{ill|Yagyū Munenori|ja|柳生宗厳}}) have visited this sacred site for their waterfall ascetic practices. Since the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, during the medieval era the shrine flourished as a central sacred site for shugendō in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}. Furthermore, the origins of the shrine trace back even further—to an era when the sacred shugendō mountains (beginning with Shugyengyō-san), Mount Hiraura, Mount Kyokuzan (the “Western Peak”), Mount Takami (the “Eastern Peak”), and the three peaks of the Kawakami Thirteen Peaks of {{ill|Hakusan|ja|白山}} (三畝) along with Gakunō-dō Mountain, Mount Karatani (洞谷山), Mount Kuroiwa, Mount Gomazan, Mount Kosumayama, Mount Kōren, Mount Butsurai, and Mount Hakuran—served as shintai (sacred mountains) with many stone altars scattered throughout, giving rise to widespread ancestral spirit worship in the regions of Ise, Iga, Yamato, and Kii. Within the shrine grounds, the Tengu Rock is said to be the site where {{ill|Emperor Hanayama|ja|花山天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}} practiced the Great Tengu Method in their aspiration to become Japan’s great tengu. == Alternate Shrine Legend 1 == According to an alternate legend, Ennyōbō Chintoku Shōnin (the shrine priest of this shrine and concurrently the priest of the Kitabatake Castle Hachiman Shrine) received a prophetic dream warning of an imminent conflagration. He learned that from Kaga Hakusan a total of eight shrines were to be established in Isshigun—seven associated with Kaga Hakusan and one including the shrine’s own sacred mountain. On the night of January 24 in the 22nd year of Tenmon (1553), he experienced a divine message in his dream: “By next February, Kaga Hakusan will be consumed by fire, so I must relocate to the land where Amaterasu resides before then.” He also observed the Big Dipper scattered across the southern sky. Though he wished to set out for Kaga immediately, the heavy winter in Hokuriku forced him to wait until the snow subsided. In early March, he visited Hakusan and engaged in a seven-day, seven-night prayer. On the seventh night, a divine message came; the following morning, he found seven gohei (sacred wand offerings) standing upright inside his travel case. Taking this as confirmation of his prophetic dream, he prostrated himself before the deity and set off home with his travel case on his back. While pausing in his hometown to visit his elderly mother, he rested his travel case on a roadside stone along the bank of {{ill|Unidegawa|ja|雲出川}} at Seto-ga-fuchi near his family castle. Suddenly, the case began to sway, and from it, seven white herons took flight. These herons were regarded as divine messengers. The number seven was seen as echoing the Big Dipper he had seen earlier. Moved by this sign, he resolved to build a Hakusan Shrine at the place where the seven white herons alighted. They descended at seven locations—the Forest of the Small Wa Wind God, the boundary at Iugawaguchi, Hatayano, Yamadano, the family castle, Takehara, and Iifukuda—and he simultaneously initiated the construction of shrine buildings at these sites. It is said that he then enshrined a portion of the spirit of Kaga Hakusan. This is known as the Legend of the Three White Heron Deities. == Alternate Shrine Legend 2 == Another tradition relates that the severed heads of {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子}}—who defied Emperor Nintoku and fled the capital in pursuit of Ise—and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女}}, were carried by retainers and enshrined here. According to this legend, their heads washed ashore at various locations such as Kawagami in Hakusan Town at Kawaguchi Seto, Hatayano in Hakusan Town, Yamato Soni Village, and Takehara Nakano in Misugi Town. Additionally, within the Fujiwara Chikakata legend—linked with Iga Kawakami (in Aoyama Town) and Mammi in Hakusan Town—a severed head is said to have floated upriver and drifted to the Kawakami area, borne by the Four Demons. These traditions reveal the complex, ancestral spirit–worship facet of the Wakamiyahachiman belief. Along the route, the {{ill|Ise Ondo|ja|伊勢音頭}} is sung with the words that if one offers a heartfelt wish to Kawakami-sama (Wakamiya-sama), even if it seems a little beyond reach, it will be granted. It is said that not only in Ise but also in Shima, Owari, Mikawa, Mino, Hida, Tōtōmi, Izu, Yamato, Ōmi, Echigo, Etchu, Echizen, Kii, and Rakuchū did the divine “sword edge” descend—bestowing blessings from both Ise and this shrine. == Ise Tea Origin Site, Fuji‑kō, and Flood Control == Kawakami is also famed as the origin of Ise tea, for along the shrine’s approach lies the site of the first tea grove (Chakubo) planted by the priest {{ill|Myōe|ja|明恵}} (also known as Kōben). The shrine priest family’s tea garden has existed since that time. In addition, the birthplace of the sixth-generation Fuji‑kō, {{ill|Shokugyōshinroku|ja|食行身禄}}, is located at the Shimizui in the parish area, and there is also the record of the previous monk Shōkō Nyūtei. Since the Edo period it has been said that three entities surpass Isshigun in renown: Shokugyōshinroku, Matsuura Takeshirō (松浦武四郎), and Wakamiyahachiman Shrine. • Saijima Hachibei; the Unide Irrigation Canal (in the Kōchaya area, where a branch shrine dedicated to the shrine’s spirit water exists); Manno Pond (founded by Prince Kōbō Daishi and Kūkai, later restored by the Hosokawa family). • Yamanaka Tametsuna; the Kōya Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Kōya, Isshigun); Matsui Magoemon; the Ise Miyagawa Irrigation Canal (with a branch shrine in Miyagawa Town, Ise City); Tendai Shinseisō Shōnin; and others who played significant roles in flood control projects (in imitation of the legend of Emperor Nintoku)—a fact that accounts for the many branch shrines dedicated to flood control deities in Owari, Mino, Iga, and Mikawa. == Return Stone == If one applies at the shrine office, prayers for the fulfillment of heartfelt wishes, longevity, good fortune, well-being of wells, and assured victory over one’s enemies are offered. In return, “sacred stones” are bestowed for enshrinement in household altars and wells, and upon the fulfillment of the prayer, a ritual is performed in which the number of stones is doubled—this “Return Stone” ritual has been carried out since ancient times. == Related Persons == 34th-generation shrine priest: the late Hirokatsu Okano, the eldest son of his wife Kei. 35th-generation shrine priest: the late Yamato Okano (brother of {{ill|Hirohiko Okano|ja|岡野弘彦}}), who was born at this shrine. {{stub}} == Enshrined Deities == 【Main Deity】 • {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}} (Great Tsubame Deity) • {{ill|Empress Iwanohime|ja|磐之媛皇后}} (Katsuragi Iwanohime) 【Secondary Deities】 • {{ill|Prince Hayabusa|ja|隼別皇子}} and {{ill|Princess Mesotori|ja|雌鳥皇女}} • Hachinaga-hime • Katsuragi Osatsuhiko • {{ill|Takeuchi no Sukune|ja|武内宿禰}}-no-mikoto • Yorihime Ogara Sukune-no-mikoto 【Co-Enshrinement】 • Second Hall: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊}}; with secondary enshrinement of {{ill|Amaterasu Omikami|ja|大日孁貴神}} • Third Hall: {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} and the Hachiman Deity ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} and his aspect {{ill|Hota Wake|ja|誉田別尊}}) 【Subsidiary Shrines (Sessha)】 • Takamiya: {{ill|Ise Ōkunidama-no-Okami|ja|伊勢大国魂大神}} (Ise Tsuhiko-no-Kami) • Branch Shrines:  – Kitofusha: honoring figures such as Kitabatake Akeno-kō, Tōdō Kōji-kō, and Inari  – Michibiki Inari: associated with successive generations of the Osaki family (including the lineage of the current shrine head’s maternal Kubokou family and, ultimately, the last head priest of Kyoto Yasaka Shrine)  – Sakayakakino Yama-no-Kami, Misaka Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Hiurasaka); Sano-ya Yama-no-Kami (originally from Nakano); Tetsurakae Yama-no-Kami (originally from Aichi); and Kanayama Hiko-no-Mikoto • Fudansha: enshrining Tentsuibun Ōkami, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾尊}}, and the purification waterfall (the source of Unidegawa); also Tsukuyomi-sha, enshrining the deity Tsukuyomi • Fudōsha: honoring {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇大神}}, {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津比売神}}, {{ill|Hayaakitsuhiko/Hayaakitsuhime|ja|速開都比売神}}, {{ill|Kibikado Shukunokami|ja|気吹戸主神}} and {{ill|Haya-sasurahime|ja|速佐須良比売神}}, along with Izanami-no-Mikoto and the Harai Waterfall (also known as Utoru-taki or the Couple’s Waterfall); additionally, Fudōson as offered by Katsura in dedication • Komiyasha: enshrining Gofuku Hime-no-Mikoto, among others; and Fujishinroku-sha, honoring Konohanasa Kuya Hime-no-Mikoto (originally from Aichi) • Tateishisha: enshrining {{ill|Yamimie-no-Kami|ja|闇龗神}} and {{ill|Takakagami|ja|高龗神}} • Manorikisha: enshrining {{ill|Tenmoku Ikka-no-Mikoto|ja|天目一箇命}} • Akagoishasha: enshrining {{ill|Nakisawa Megami|ja|泣沢女神}} and {{ill|Iwanagahime|ja|磐長姫尊}} == Main Treasures == The lantern stone associated with the tradition of Japanese filial piety (the “Twenty-Four Filial Exemplars”)—attributed to the tutelary deity Yamamoto Mokubei—is located in Nakamura Kurihara. The related lantern, once positioned before the Ota-ya, now stands in front of the Nino Torii parking lot, and the gourd field bestowed by the Tōdō domain lord is situated in Sakamoto. Also featured are the guardian dogs of the Komiyasha and those of the former Kitofusha (which enshrined deities such as {{ill|Kitabatake Akeno|ja|北畠顕能}} and {{ill|Tōdō Kōji|ja|藤堂高次}}). == External Links == * {{Official website|name=Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine Official Website|https://www.kawakamisanwakamiyahachiman.jp|format=flash}} * [https://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2929.html Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜Tourist Spot｜Tourism Mie] * [https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/918/ Kawakami San Wakamiyahachiman Shrine｜See & Experience｜Official Tsu City Tourism Association website ...] {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tsu City]] [[Category:Buildings in Tsu City]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Iwanaga Faith]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:川上山若宮八幡宮]]

revision.text:
{{複数の問題 |出典の明記 = 2024年3月 |独自研究 = 2024年3月 |言葉を濁さない = 2024年3月 |Wikify = 2024年3月 |内容過剰 = 2024年3月 }} {{神社 | 名称=川上山若宮八幡宮 | 所在地=[[三重県]][[津市]]美杉町川上3498 | 位置={{Coord|34|27|49.3|N|136|15|57.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=仁徳天皇　磐之媛皇后 | 創建=履中天皇代(西暦400年～406年) | 社格=無格社　本殿合祀社は村社 | 例祭=10月19日 | 本殿=神明造 | 画像=[[画像:川上山若宮八幡宮.JPG|200px]]
拝殿 | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie Prefecture|label=川上山若宮八幡宮|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''川上山若宮八幡宮'''（かわかみさんわかみやはちまんぐう）は、[[三重県]][[津市]]美杉町にある[[若宮八幡宮]]。愛称は川上様・若宮様・川上八幡様・若宮八幡様など。日本最古の若宮八幡宮である。 == 社伝 == 社伝によると磐之媛皇后の御領地岩野郷で、[[仁徳天皇]]はこの郷の開拓を行った為に[[履中天皇]]の御代（西暦5世紀初）に郷民が、[[仁徳天皇]]及び磐之媛皇后の御遺徳を偲んで、[[伊勢平野]]を見下ろす修験業山の麓この川上の清地を選んで御社を設け、その御魂を祀ったのが日本最古の当若宮八幡宮であると伝えられている。 伊勢国司の[[北畠家]]から非常に崇敬された社であり、この社を崇敬する余り隣村の多気に城を造り、神領を献じた上で鳥屋尾家老を常置の奉行とし、由緒ある雀部（笹部）勘太夫岡野本家を永代の神主として新ためて太夫号を授けたとされている。当初太夫は十二、藤堂藩の時は八存在した。陰陽管理といわれ勘太夫神主は伊之助、勘太夫などの大相撲式守の任免権を持っていた。 また北畠氏は当社の尊厳を保つ為には岡野神主家の住宅以降は閂関を設けて一般の参拝を禁じたとされている。春秋の大祭に伊勢国司自らが参詣して新政は先ず神意に計って実施するのを常とした。江戸時代以前、神仏習合時代には多くの付属寺院を有した。 [[平宗清]]の末裔、日置、福地、北村の伊賀柘植三方は氏神として信仰した。 [[伊賀市|伊賀]]（[[名張市]]）一ノ井出身道観長者、小太郎（八幡）長者親子月参、祈願、仇討ち結願のお宮でもある。 藤堂藩主も祈願所とし春秋大祭には必ず参拝奉幣した。 [[役小角|役行者]]や大谷刑部（[[大谷吉継]]）、剣豪の[[鹿島新當流]]の[[塚原卜伝]]が[[鎖鎌]]之秘宝（[[鎖鎌術]]）を生み出すとともに境内において[[北畠具教]]に一の太刀を伝授、陰流愛洲移香斎（[[愛洲久忠]]）、柳生重永、新陰流柳生石舟斎（[[柳生宗厳]]）などが滝行に訪れた霊場である。 [[奈良時代]]以降、中世期には[[伊勢国]]に於ける[[修験道]]の中心的聖地として隆盛した歴史もある。 又神社発祥の根源は更に古く修験業山はじめ、平倉峰、局山（西の嶽山）、高見山（東の嶽山）、三峰（三畝）山、（岳の洞）学能堂山、唐谷（洞谷）山　、黒岩山、護摩山、小須磨山、黄連山、仏来山、白山（しらやま）の川上十三峰を神体山として多くの磐座が点在し広く伊勢、伊賀、大和、紀伊の地域祖霊信仰に発生していることによる。 境内にある天狗岩は[[花山天皇]]、[[後醍醐天皇]]、我は日ノ本の大天狗ならんと大天狗法を修されたといわれる。 === 社伝異説1 === 円乗坊珍徳上人（当宮社僧にして併せて北家城八幡神社社僧）霊夢により焼出の危機を聞き加賀白山より一志郡に七座と当宮神体山の一白山含め八座を勧請。 天文22年(1553)正月２４日の夜、「明年２月加賀白山は火災で焼失してしまうから、それまでに天照大神のいます地に移りたい｡」 という夢のお告げを感じ、北斗七星が南方の空に点々と輝くのを見た。円乗坊は夢告にこたえてすぐにも加賀へ行きたいと思ったが、 北陸は雪のさなかであるので雪が少なくなるのを待って、３月上旬、白山に詣でて七日七夜の祈願をこめた。七日目の夜、神様のお告げがあり、 翌朝みると笈の中に御幣が７本立っていた。これこそ霊夢の験であると感じて、神前にぬかずいて後、笈を背負って帰国の途についた。 故郷へ立ち寄って年老いた母にあうために家城の近く[[雲出川]]の瀬戸ケ淵の岸で路傍の石に笈をおろし暫し疲れをいやしていた時、 にわかに笈が前後にゆれ動いて中から七羽の白鷺が飛び立った。白鷺は神の使者である。 それが七羽というのは、さきに霊夢で見た南方に輝く北斗七星と同じ意味であると感じて、その七羽の白鷺がおり立ったところへ白山神社を建てようと念願した。 白鷺がおりた所は小倭の風神の森、井生川口の境、ハ対野、山田野、家城、竹原、飯福田の七ケ所で、この地に同時に社殿の建立にとりかかった。 そして､加賀白山の御分霊を奉斎したと伝えられている。白鷺三様伝説。 === 社伝異説2 === 同神社には[[仁徳天皇]]に背き、都から出奔して伊勢の地を目指し白山町川口瀬戸が淵、白山町八対野、大和曾爾村、美杉町竹原中野等で斬首された[[隼別皇子]]と[[雌鳥皇女]]の首が流れ着き、臣下により運ばれそれをお祀りしたとの伝承や伊賀川上（青山町）や白山町真見の藤原千方伝説の中でも斬首された首が川を上ってこの川上の地に漂着した、四鬼により運ばれたとの伝承（伊賀市上野東町菅原神社天神祭）もあり、若宮八幡信仰の御霊信仰的で複雑な一面も存在する。 道中[[伊勢音頭]]には願掛けするなら川上様（若宮様）心こもれる願いなら少々無理でも聞かしゃんすと歌われ、お陰参りでは伊勢の国はもとより志摩、尾張、三河、美濃，飛騨、遠江、伊豆、大和、近江、大和、越後、越中、越前、紀州、洛中と伊勢の剣先とともに当宮の剣先が降ったと謂われている。 == 伊勢茶発祥地　伊勢茶発祥地　冨士講　治水 == 川上は[[明恵]]（高弁）上人が[[伊勢茶]]を植えた初茶窪が参道にあり伊勢茶発祥地といわれる。神主家茶園はこの時から存在する。 富士講第六世[[食行身禄]]の産湯も氏子区域清水井にあり、更に前行正古入定跡もある。一志郡に過ぎたるものは3つあり　　身禄　松浦武四郎　若宮八幡宮と江戸時代より謂われている。 西島八兵衛　雲出用水（高茶屋地区に当宮不断社分霊水分神社あり）　満濃池(弘法太子空海創始　細川家修復時） 　山中為綱　高野用水（一志町高野分霊あり）　松井孫右衛門　伊勢宮川用水（伊勢市宮川町分霊あり）　天台真盛宗真盛上人　大井井生などの治水事業の節にも大きな信仰を寄せた（仁徳帝の故事に肖り）尾張、美濃、伊賀，三河に分祀が多いのも治水の神としての信仰ゆえにである。 == お返し石 == 社務所に申し出られれば心願成就、長寿、開運、井戸平安、敵倍必勝のため祈願の上、各宅の神棚、井戸などにまつるために「御石」を授与し、祈願成就の暁には個数を倍にして返す「お返し石」の儀礼が古くから執り行われている。 == 血縁者 == 第34代神主：故岡野弘賢　けい妻長男　第35代神主：故岡野倭文彦　兄[[　岡野弘彦]]　当宮出生 {{節stub}} == 祭神 == 【主神】　[[仁徳天皇]](大鷦鷯尊)　磐之媛皇后(葛城磐之媛) 【配祀】 [[隼別皇子#:~:text%3D%E4%BB%81%E5%BE%B3%E5%A4%A9%E7%9A%87%E3%81%AF-%2C%E9%9B%8C%E9%B3%A5%E7%9A%87%E5%A5%B3%2C-%EF%BC%88%E3%82%81%E3%81%A8%E3%82%8A%E3%81%AE|隼別皇子、雌鳥皇女]]、髪長比売、葛城襲津彦命、[[武内宿禰]]命、許勢小柄宿禰命 【合祀】　第二殿　[[スサノオ|素盞嗚尊]]、配祀 [[天照大神|大日孁貴神]]　第三殿　[[神功皇后]]、八幡神([[応神天皇]]、[[応神天皇|誉田別尊]])　　 【別宮扱の摂社】 ・高宮　伊勢大国魂大神（伊勢津彦神'''）''' 末社 。北藤社　北畠顕能公　藤堂高次公　稲荷 。導稲荷　南朝忠臣　度会神主尾崎氏累代御霊　現宮司実母久保家の母生家尾崎家　最後京都八坂神社宮司絶家により ・酒屋垣内山ノ神　味坂比賣命　元非浦坂元にあり　佐野家山ノ神　元中野にあり　鉄羅界山ノ神　元相地にあり　金山比古命 ・不断社　天水分大神　[[イザナギ|伊弉諾尊]]　禊滝（雲出川本流水源）月読社　ツキヨミの命 ・不動社　[[オオヤマツミ|大山祇大神]]　[[瀬織津姫|瀬織津比売神]]　[[ハヤアキツヒコ・ハヤアキツヒメ|速開都比売神]]　[[気吹戸主神　]][[速佐須良比売神]]　伊弉冉尊　祓滝（魚留滝、夫婦滝）　　　　　　　　不動尊　勝浦某奉納 ・小宮社　呉服比賣命　他　冨士身禄社　このはなさくやひめのみこと　元相地にあり ・立石社　[[淤加美神|闇龗神]]　[[淤加美神|高龗神]] ・眼力社　[[天目一箇命]] ・赤子石社　[[泣沢女神]]　[[イワナガヒメ|磐長姫尊]] == 主な宝物 == 日本孝子伝（廿四孝）にいう氏子山本杢兵衛の灯明石は中村栗原にあり，関係する灯篭はかつては太田屋前にあったが、現在は二ノ鳥居駐車場前にあり、藤堂藩主より頂しひょうたん田は坂本にある。 小宮社狛犬　旧北藤社（祭神[[北畠顕能]]　[[藤堂高次]]）狛犬 == 外部リンク == * {{Official website|name=川上山若宮八幡宮のホームページ|https://www.kawakamisanwakamiyahachiman.jp|format=flash}} * [https://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2929.html 川上山若宮八幡神社｜観光スポット｜観光三重] * [https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/918/ 川上山若宮八幡宮｜観る・体験する｜津市観光協会公式サイト ...] {{DEFAULTSORT:かわかみさんわかみやはちまんくう}} [[Category:八幡宮]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:津市の歴史]] [[Category:津市の建築物]] [[Category:仁徳天皇]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[川上山若宮八幡宮]] to [[Kawakami Yamawaka Hachiman Shrine]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{複数の問題 |出典の明記 = 2024年3月 |独自研究 = 2024年3月 |言葉を濁さない = 2024年3月 |Wikify = 2024年3月 |内容過剰 = 2024年3月 }} {{神社 | 名称=川上山若宮八幡宮 | 所在地=[[三重県]][[津市]]美杉町川上3498 | 位置={{Coord|34|27|49.3|N|136|15|57.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=仁徳天皇　磐之媛皇后 | 創建=履中天皇代(西暦400年～406年) | 社格=無格社　本殿合祀社は村社 | 例祭=10月19日 | 本殿=神明造 | 画像=[[画像:川上山若宮八幡宮.JPG|200px]]
拝殿 | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie Prefecture|label=川上山若宮八幡宮|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''川上山若宮八幡宮'''（かわかみさんわかみやはちまんぐう）は、[[三重県]][[津市]]美杉町にある[[若宮八幡宮]]。愛称は川上様・若宮様・川上八幡様・若宮八幡様など。日本最古の若宮八幡宮である。 == 社伝 == 社伝によると磐之媛皇后の御領地岩野郷で、[[仁徳天皇]]はこの郷の開拓を行った為に[[履中天皇]]の御代（西暦5世紀初）に郷民が、[[仁徳天皇]]及び磐之媛皇后の御遺徳を偲んで、[[伊勢平野]]を見下ろす修験業山の麓この川上の清地を選んで御社を設け、その御魂を祀ったのが日本最古の当若宮八幡宮であると伝えられている。 伊勢国司の[[北畠家]]から非常に崇敬された社であり、この社を崇敬する余り隣村の多気に城を造り、神領を献じた上で鳥屋尾家老を常置の奉行とし、由緒ある雀部（笹部）勘太夫岡野本家を永代の神主として新ためて太夫号を授けたとされている。当初太夫は十二、藤堂藩の時は八存在した。陰陽管理といわれ勘太夫神主は伊之助、勘太夫などの大相撲式守の任免権を持っていた。 また北畠氏は当社の尊厳を保つ為には岡野神主家の住宅以降は閂関を設けて一般の参拝を禁じたとされている。春秋の大祭に伊勢国司自らが参詣して新政は先ず神意に計って実施するのを常とした。江戸時代以前、神仏習合時代には多くの付属寺院を有した。 [[平宗清]]の末裔、日置、福地、北村の伊賀柘植三方は氏神として信仰した。 [[伊賀市|伊賀]]（[[名張市]]）一ノ井出身道観長者、小太郎（八幡）長者親子月参、祈願、仇討ち結願のお宮でもある。 藤堂藩主も祈願所とし春秋大祭には必ず参拝奉幣した。 [[役小角|役行者]]や大谷刑部（[[大谷吉継]]）、剣豪の[[鹿島新當流]]の[[塚原卜伝]]が[[鎖鎌]]之秘宝（[[鎖鎌術]]）を生み出すとともに境内において[[北畠具教]]に一の太刀を伝授、陰流愛洲移香斎（[[愛洲久忠]]）、柳生重永、新陰流柳生石舟斎（[[柳生宗厳]]）などが滝行に訪れた霊場である。 [[奈良時代]]以降、中世期には[[伊勢国]]に於ける[[修験道]]の中心的聖地として隆盛した歴史もある。 又神社発祥の根源は更に古く修験業山はじめ、平倉峰、局山（西の嶽山）、高見山（東の嶽山）、三峰（三畝）山、（岳の洞）学能堂山、唐谷（洞谷）山　、黒岩山、護摩山、小須磨山、黄連山、仏来山、白山（しらやま）の川上十三峰を神体山として多くの磐座が点在し広く伊勢、伊賀、大和、紀伊の地域祖霊信仰に発生していることによる。 境内にある天狗岩は[[花山天皇]]、[[後醍醐天皇]]、我は日ノ本の大天狗ならんと大天狗法を修されたといわれる。 === 社伝異説1 === 円乗坊珍徳上人（当宮社僧にして併せて北家城八幡神社社僧）霊夢により焼出の危機を聞き加賀白山より一志郡に七座と当宮神体山の一白山含め八座を勧請。 天文22年(1553)正月２４日の夜、「明年２月加賀白山は火災で焼失してしまうから、それまでに天照大神のいます地に移りたい｡」 という夢のお告げを感じ、北斗七星が南方の空に点々と輝くのを見た。円乗坊は夢告にこたえてすぐにも加賀へ行きたいと思ったが、 北陸は雪のさなかであるので雪が少なくなるのを待って、３月上旬、白山に詣でて七日七夜の祈願をこめた。七日目の夜、神様のお告げがあり、 翌朝みると笈の中に御幣が７本立っていた。これこそ霊夢の験であると感じて、神前にぬかずいて後、笈を背負って帰国の途についた。 故郷へ立ち寄って年老いた母にあうために家城の近く[[雲出川]]の瀬戸ケ淵の岸で路傍の石に笈をおろし暫し疲れをいやしていた時、 にわかに笈が前後にゆれ動いて中から七羽の白鷺が飛び立った。白鷺は神の使者である。 それが七羽というのは、さきに霊夢で見た南方に輝く北斗七星と同じ意味であると感じて、その七羽の白鷺がおり立ったところへ白山神社を建てようと念願した。 白鷺がおりた所は小倭の風神の森、井生川口の境、ハ対野、山田野、家城、竹原、飯福田の七ケ所で、この地に同時に社殿の建立にとりかかった。 そして､加賀白山の御分霊を奉斎したと伝えられている。白鷺三様伝説。 === 社伝異説2 === 同神社には[[仁徳天皇]]に背き、都から出奔して伊勢の地を目指し白山町川口瀬戸が淵、白山町八対野、大和曾爾村、美杉町竹原中野等で斬首された[[隼別皇子]]と[[雌鳥皇女]]の首が流れ着き、臣下により運ばれそれをお祀りしたとの伝承や伊賀川上（青山町）や白山町真見の藤原千方伝説の中でも斬首された首が川を上ってこの川上の地に漂着した、四鬼により運ばれたとの伝承（伊賀市上野東町菅原神社天神祭）もあり、若宮八幡信仰の御霊信仰的で複雑な一面も存在する。 道中[[伊勢音頭]]には願掛けするなら川上様（若宮様）心こもれる願いなら少々無理でも聞かしゃんすと歌われ、お陰参りでは伊勢の国はもとより志摩、尾張、三河、美濃，飛騨、遠江、伊豆、大和、近江、大和、越後、越中、越前、紀州、洛中と伊勢の剣先とともに当宮の剣先が降ったと謂われている。 == 伊勢茶発祥地　伊勢茶発祥地　冨士講　治水 == 川上は[[明恵]]（高弁）上人が[[伊勢茶]]を植えた初茶窪が参道にあり伊勢茶発祥地といわれる。神主家茶園はこの時から存在する。 富士講第六世[[食行身禄]]の産湯も氏子区域清水井にあり、更に前行正古入定跡もある。一志郡に過ぎたるものは3つあり　　身禄　松浦武四郎　若宮八幡宮と江戸時代より謂われている。
